Steadfastness; constancy; fixedness; as the firmness of a purpose or resolution; the firmness of a man, or of his courage; firmness of mind or soul.
Certainty; soundness; as the firmness of notions or opinions.
first: Advanced before or further than any other in progression; foremost in place; as the first man in a marching company or troop is the man that precedes all the rest. Hence,
Preceding all others in the order of time. Adam was the first man. Cain was the first murderer. Monday was the first day of January.
Preceding all others in numbers or a progressive series; the ordinal of one; as, 1 is the first number.
Preceding all others in rank, dignity or excellence. Demosthenes was the first orator of Greece. Burke was one of the first geniuses of his age. Give God the first place in your affections.
Before any thing else in the order of time. Adam was first formed, then Eve. 7 Timothy 2:713.
first-begotten: First produced; the eldest of children.
first-born: First brought forth; first in the order of nativity; eldest; as the first- born son.
Most excellent; most distinguished or exalted. Christ is called the first-born of every creature. Colossians 1:15.&
The eldest child; the first in the order of birth. The first-born of the poor are the most wretched. Isaiah 14:30. The first-born of death is the most terrible death. Job 18:73.
first-fruit: The fruit or produce first matured and collected in any season. Of these the Jews made an oblation to God, as an acknowledgment of his sovereign dominion.
The first profits of any thing. In the church of England, the profits of every spiritual benefice for the first year.
The first or earliest effect of any thing, in a good or bad sense; as the first-fruits of grace in the heart, or the first-fruits of vice.
first-fruits: The fruit or produce first matured and collected in any season. Of these the Jews made an oblation to God, as an acknowledgment of his sovereign dominion.
The first profits of any thing. In the church of England, the profits of every spiritual benefice for the first year.
The first or earliest effect of any thing, in a good or bad sense; as the first-fruits of grace in the heart, or the first-fruits of vice.
firstling: First produced; as firstling males. Deuteronomy 15:19.
The first produce or offspring; applied to beasts; as the firstlings of cattle.
The thing first thought or done. The very firstlings of my heart shall be the firstlings of my hand.
first-rate: Of the highest excellence; preeminent; as a first-rate scholar or painter.
Being of the largest size; as a first-rate ship.
fisc: The treasury of a prince or state; hence to confiscate to take the good of a criminal and appropriate then to the public treasure.
fiscal: Pertaining to the public treasury or revenue. The fiscal arrangement of government.
Revenue; the income of a prince or state.
fish: An animal that lives in water. Fish is a general name for a class of animals subsisting in water, which were distributed by Linne into six orders. They breathe by means of gills, swim by the aid of fins, and are oviparous. Some of them have the skeleton bony, and others cartilaginous. Most of the former have the opening of the gills closed by a peculiar covering, called the gill-lid; many of the latter have no gill-lid, and are hence said to breathe through apertures. Cetaceous animals, as the whale and dolphin, are, in popular language, called fishes, and have been so classed by some naturalists; but they breathe by lungs, and are viviparous, like quadrupeds. The term fish has been also extended to other aquatic animals, such as shell-fish, lobstersWe use fish, in the singular, for fishes in general or the whole race.
The flesh of fish, used as food. But we usually apply flesh to land animals.
To attempt to catch fish; to be employed in taking fish, by any means, as by angling or drawing nets.
To attempt or seek to obtain by artifice, or indirectly to seek to draw forth; as, to fish for compliments.
To search by raking or sweeping; as, to fish the jakes for papers.
fisher: One who is employed in catching fish.
fisherboat: A boat employed in catching fish.
fisherman: One whose occupation is to catch fish.
A ship or vessel employed in the business of taking fish, as in the cod and whale fishery.
fishery: A place for catching fish with nets or hooks, as the banks of Newfoundland, the coast of England or Scotland, or on the banks of rivers.
fishful: Abounding with fish; as a fishful pond.
fishgig: An instrument used for striking fish at sea, consisting of a staff with barbed prongs, and a line fastened just above the prongs.
fizgig: An instrument used for striking fish at sea, consisting of a staff with barbed prongs, and a line fastened just above the prongs.
A fire-work, made of powder rolled up in a paper.
fishing: Attempting to catch fish; searching; seeking to draw forth by artifice or indirectly; adding a piece of timber to a mast or spar to strengthen it.
The art or practice of catching fish.
fishing-frog: The toad-fish, or Lophius, whose head is larger than the body.
fishing-place: A place where fishes are caught with seines; a convenient place for fishing; a fishery.
fishkettle: A kettle made long for boiling fish whole.
fishmarket: A place where fish are exposed for sale.
fishmeal: A meal of fish; diet on fish; abstemious diet.
fishmonger: A seller of fish; a dealer in fish.
fishpond: A pond in which fishes are bred and kept.
fishroom: An apartment in a ship between the after-hold and the spirit room.
fishspear: A spear for taking fish by stabbing them.
fishwife: A woman that cries fish for sale.
fishy: Inhabited by fish; as the fishy flood.
Having the qualities of fish; like fish; as a fishy form; a fishy taste or smell.
fissile: That may be split, cleft or divided in the direction of the grain, or of natural joints. This crystal is a pellucid fissile stone.
fissility: The quality of admitting to be cleft.
fissiped: An animal whose toes are separate, or not connected by a membrane.
fissure: A cleft; a narrow chasm made by the parting of any substance; a longitudinal opening; as the fissure of a rock.
In surgery, a crack or slit in a bone, either transversely or longitudinally, by means of external force.
In anatomy, a deep, narrow sulcus, or depression, dividing the anterior and middle lobes of the cerebrum on each side.
To cleave; to divide; to crack or fracture.
fist: The hand clinched; the hand with the fingers doubled into the palm.
fisticuffs: Blows or a combat with the fist; a boxing.
fistula: Properly, a pipe; a wind instrument of music, originally a reed.

A surgery, a deep, narrow and callous ulcer, generally arising from abscesses. It differs from a sinus, in being callous. Fistula lachrymalis, a fistula of the lachrymal sac, a disorder accompanied with a flowing of tears.
fistular: Hollow, like a pipe or reed.
fistulate: To become a pipe or fistula.
To make hollow like a pipe.
fistuliform: Being in round hollow columns, as a mineral. Stalactite often occurs fistuliform.
fistulous: Having the form or nature of a fistula; as a fistulous ulcer.
fit: The invasion, exacerbation or paroxysm of a disease. We apply the word to the return of an ague, after intermission, as a cold fit. We apply it to the first attack, or to the return of other diseases, as a fit of the gout or stone; and in general, to a disease however continued, as a fit of sickness.
A sudden and violent attack of disorder, in which the body is often convulsed, and sometimes senseless; as a fit of apoplexy or epilepsy; hysteric fits.
Any short return after intermission; a turn; a period or interval. He moves by fits and starts. By fits my swelling grief appears.
A temporary affection or attack; as a fit of melancholy, or of grief; a fit of pleasure.
fitful: Varied by paroxysms; full of fits.
fitly: Suitably; properly; with propriety. A maxim fitly applied.
fitness: Suitableness; adaptedness; adaptation; as the fitness of things to their use.
Propriety; meetness; justness; reasonableness; as the fitness of measures or laws.
Preparation; qualification; as a student’s fitness for college.
Convenience; the state of being fit.
fitter: One who makes fit or suitable; one who adapts; one who prepares.
fitting: Making suitable; adapting; preparing; qualifying; providing with.
fitz: Norm. fites, fuz or fiz, a son, is used in names, as in Fitzherbert, Fitzroy, Carlovitz.
five: Four and one added; the half of ten; as five men; five loaves. Like other adjectives, it is often used as a noun. Five of them were wise, and five were foolish. Matthew 25:2.
fivebar: Having five bars; as a fivebarred gate.
fivebarred: Having five bars; as a fivebarred gate.
fivefold: In fives; consisting of five in one; five-double; five times repeated.
fiveleafed: Having five leaves; as fiveleafed clover, or cinquefoil.
fives: A kind of play with a ball.
A disease of horses, resembling the strangles.
fix: To make stable; to set or establish immovably. The universe is governed by fixed laws.
To set or place permanently; to establish. The prince fixed his residence at York. The seat of our government is fixed at Washington in the district of Columbia. Some men have no fixed opinions.
To make fast; to fasten; to attach firmly; as, to fix a cord or line to a hook.
To set or place steadily; to direct, as the eye, without moving it; to fasten. The gentleman fixed his eyes on the speaker, and addressed him with firmness.
To set or direct steadily, without wandering; as, to fix the attention. The preacher fixes the attention of his audience, or the hearers fix their attention on the preacher.
fixable: That may be fixed, established, or rendered firm.
fixation: Stability; firmness; steadiness; a state of being established; as fixation in matters of religion.
Residence in a certain place; or a place of residence. To light, created in the first day, God gave no certain place or fixation.
That firm state of a body which resists evaporation or volatilization by heat; as the fixation of gold or other metals.
The act or process of ceasing to be fluid and becoming firm; state of being fixed.
fixed: Settled; established; firm; fast; stable. Fixed air, an invisible and permanently elastic fluid, heavier than common air and fatal to animal life, produced from the combustion of carbonaceous bodies, as wood or charcoal, and by artificial processes; called also aerial acid, cretaceous acid, and more generally, carbonic acid. Fixed bodies, are those which bear a high heat without evaporation or volatilization. Fixed stars, are such stars as always retain the same apparent position and distance with respect to each other, and are thus distinguished from planets and comets, which are revolving bodies. Fixed oils, such as are obtained by simple pressure, and are not readily volatilized; so called in distinction from volatile or essential oils.
fixedly: Firmly; in a settled or established manner; steadfastly.
fixedness: A state of being fixed; stability; firmness; steadfastness; as a fixedness in religion or politics; fixedness of opinion on any subject.
The state of a body which resists evaporation or volatilization by heat; as the fixedness of gold.
fixity: Fixedness; coherence of parts; that property of bodies by which they resist dissipation by heat.
fixture: Fixedness; firm pressure; as the fixture of the foot.

That which is fixed to a building; any appendage or part of the furniture of a house which is fixed to it, as by nails, screws, and which the tenant cannot legally take away, when he removes to another house.
flabbiness: A soft, flexible state of a substance, which renders it easily movable and yielding to pressure.
flabby: Soft; yielding to the touch and easily moved or shaken; easily bent; hanging loose by its own weight; as flabby flesh.
flaccid: Soft and weak; limber; lax; drooping; hanging down by its own weight; yielding to pressure for want of firmness and stiffness; as a flaccid muscle; flaccid flesh.
flaccidness: Laxity; limberness: want of firmness or stiffness.
flaccidity: Laxity; limberness: want of firmness or stiffness.
flag: To hang loose without stiffness; to bend down as flexible bodies; to be loose and yielding; as the flagging sails.
To grow spiritless or dejected; to droop; to grow languid; as, the spirits flag.
To grow weak; to lose vigor; as, the strength flags.
To become dull or languid. The pleasures of the town begin to flag.
To let fall into feebleness; to suffer to drop; as, to flag the wings.
flagworm: A worm or grub found among flags and sedge.
flagelet: A small flute; a small wind instrument of music.
flagellant: One who whips himself in religious discipline. The flagellants were a fanatical sect which arose in Italy, AD. 1260, who maintained that flagellation was of equal virtue with baptism and the sacrament. They walked in procession with shoulders bare, and whipped themselves till the blood ran down their bodies, to obtain the mercy of God, and appease his wrath against the vices of the age.
flagellation: A beating or whipping; a flogging; the discipline of the scourge.
flagging: Growing weak; drooping; laying with flat stones.
flaggy: Weak in taste; insipid; as a flaggy apple.
flagitious: Deeply criminal; grossly wicked; villainous; atrocious; scandalous; as a flagitious action or crime.
Guilty of enormous crimes; corrupt; wicked; as a flagitious person.
Marked or infected with scandalous crimes or vices; as flagitious times.
flagon: A vessel with a narrow mouth, used for holding and conveying liquors. Stay me with flagons, comfort me with apples; for I am sick of love.
flagrancy: A burning; great heat; inflammation. Obs. Lust causeth a flagrancy in the eyes.
Excess; enormity; as the flagrancy of a crime.
flagrant: Burning; ardent; eager; as flagrant desires.
Glowing; red; flushed. See Sapho, at her toilet’s greasy task, Then issuing flagrant to an evening mask.
Red; inflamed. The beadle’s lash still flagrant on their back.
Flaming in notice; glaring; notorious; enormous; as a flagrant crime.
flail: An instrument for thrashing or beating corn from the ear.
flake: A small collection of snow, as it falls from the clouds or from the air; a little bunch or cluster of snowy crystals, such as fall in still moderate weather. This is a flake, lock or flock of snow.
A platform of hurdles, or small sticks made fast or interwoven, supported by stanchions, on which cod-fish is dried.
A layer or stratum; as a flake of flesh or tallow. Job 41:23.
A collection or little particle of fire, or of combustible matter on fire, separated and flying off.
Any scaly matter in layers; any mass cleaving off in scales. Little flakes of scurf.
flaky: Consisting of flakes or locks; consisting of small loose masses.
Lying in flakes; consisting of layers, or cleaving off in layers.
flam: A freak or whim; also, a falsehood; a lie; an illusory pretext; deception; delusion. Lies immortalized and consigned over as a perpetual abuse and flam upon posterity.
To deceive with falsehood; to delude.
flambeau: flam’bo. A light or luminary made of thick wicks covered with wax, and used in the streets at night, at illuminations, and in processions. Flambeaus are made square, and usually consist of four wicks or branches, near an inch thick, and about three feet long, composed of coarse hempen yarn, half twisted.
flame: A blaze; burning vapor; vapor in combustion; or according to modern chimistry, hydrogen or any inflammable gas, in a state of combustion, and naturally ascending in a stream from burning bodies being specifically lighter than common air.
Heat of passion; tumult; combustion; blaze; violent contention. One jealous, tattling mischief-maker will set a whole village in a flame.
Ardor of temper or imagination; brightness of fancy; vigor of thought. Great are their faults, and glorious is their flame.
Ardor of inclination; warmth of affection. Smit with the love of kindred arts we came, And met congenial, mingling flame with flame.
flamecolor: Bright color, as that of flame.
flamecolored: Of the color of flame; of a bright yellow color.
flamen: In ancient Rome, a priest. Originally there were three priests so called; the Flamen Dialis, consecrated to Jupiter; Flamen Martialis, sacred to Mars; and Flamen Quirinalis, who superintended the rites of Quirinus or Romulus.
flaming: Burning in flame. 2 .a. Bright; red. Also, violent; vehement; as a flaming harangue.
A bursting out in a flame.
flamingly: Most brightly; with great show or vehemence.
flamingo: A fowl constituting the genus Phoenicopterus, of the grallic order. The beak is naked, toothed, and bent as if broken; the feet palmated and four-toed. This fowl resembles the heron in shape, but is entirely red, except the quill-fethers. It is a native of Africa and America.
flammability: The quality of admitting to be set on fire, or enkindled into a flame or blaze; inflammability.
flammable: Capable of being enkindled into flame.
flammation: The act of setting on flame. The three last words are little used. Instead of them are used the compounds, inflammable, inflammability.
flamy: Having the nature of flame; as flamy matter.
flank: The fleshy or muscular part of the side of an animal, between the ribs and the hip. Hence,
The side of an army, or of any division of an army, as of a brigade, regiment or battalion. To attack an enemy in flank, is to attack them on the side.

In fortification, that part of a bastion which reaches from the curtain to the face, and defends the opposite face, the flank and the curtain; or it is a line drawn from the extremity of the face towards the inside of the work.
To attack the side or flank of an army or body of troops; or to place troops so as to command or attack the flank.
To post so as to overlook or command on the side; as, to flank a passage.
flanked: Attacked on the side; covered or commanded on the flank.
flanker: A fortification projecting so as to command the side of an assailing body.
flannel: A soft nappy woolen cloth of loose texture.
flap: Any thing broad and limber that hangs loose, or is easily moved. A cartilaginous flap on the opening of the larynx. We say, the flap of a garment, the flap of the ear, the flap of a hat.
The motion of any thing broad and loose, or a stroke with it.
The flaps, a disease in the lips of horses.
To beat with a flap. Yet let me flap this bug with gilded wings./ .
To move something broad; as, to flap the wings.
flapdragon: A play in which they catch raisins out of burning brandy, and extinguishing them by closing the mouth, eat them.
flapped: Struck with something broad, let down; having the brim fallen, as a flapped hat.
flapping: Striking; beating; moving something broad; as flapping wings. The ducks run flapping and fluttering.
flare: To waver; to flutter; to burn with an unsteady light; as, the candle flares, that is the light wanders from its natural course.
To flutter with splendid show; to be loose and waving as a showy thing. With ribbons pendant flaring ‘bout her head.
To glitter with transient luster. But speech alone doth vanish like a flaring thing.
To glitter with painful splendor. When the sun begins to fling his flaring beams.
To be exposed to too much light. I cannot stay flaring in sunshine all the day.
flaring: Burning with a wavering light; fluttering; glittering; showy.
Opening; widening outward; as a flaring fireplace.
flash: A sudden burst of light; a flood of light instantaneously appearing and disappearing; as a flash of lightning.
A sudden burst of flame and light; as instantaneous blaze; as the flash of a gun.
A sudden burst, as of wit or merriment; as a flash of wit; a flash of joy or mirth. His companions recollect no instance of premature wit, no striking sentiment, no flash of fancy - .
Ashort, transient state. The Persians and Macedonians had it for a flash.
A body of water driven by violence.
flasher: Aman of more appearance of wit than reality.
flashily: With empty show; with a sudden glare; without solidity of wit or thought.
flashing: Bursting forth as a flood of light, or of flame and light, or as wit, mirth or joy.
flashy: Showy, but empty; dazzling for a moment, but not solid; as flashy wit.
Showy; gay; as a flashy dress.
Insipid; vapid; without taste or spirit; as food or drink.
flask: A kind of bottle; as a flask of wine or oil.
flasket: A vessel in which viands are served up.
flat: Having an even surface, without risings or indentures, hills or valleys; as flat land.
Horizontal; level; without inclination; as a flat roof; or with a moderate inclination or slope; for we often apply the word to the roof of a house that is not steep, though inclined.
Prostrate; lying the whole length on the ground. He fell or lay flat on the ground.
Not elevated or erect; fallen. Cease t’admire, and beauty’s plumes fall flat.
Level with the ground; totally fallen. What ruins kingdoms, and lays cities flat.
flat-bottomed: Having a flat bottom, as a boat, or a moat in fortification.
flatlong: With the flat side downward; not edgewise.
flatly: Peremptorily; positively; downright. He flatly refused his aid.
flatness: Evenness of surface; levelness; equality of surface.
Want of relief or prominence; as the flatness of a figure in sculpture.
Deadness; vapidness; insipidity; as the flatness of cider or beer.
Dejection of fortune; low state. The flatness of my misery.
Dejection of mind; a low state of the spirits; depression; want of life.
flatted: Made flat; rendered even on the surface; also, rendered vapid or insipid.
flatten: To make flat; to reduce to an equal or even surface; to level.
To beat down to the ground; to lay flat.
To make vapid or insipid; to render stale.
To depress; to deject, as the spirits; to dispirit.
In music, to reduce, as sound; to render less acute or sharp.
flatter: The person or thing by which any thing is flattened.

To soothe by praise; to gratify self-love by praise or obsequiousness; to please a person by applause or favorable notice, by respectful attention, or by any thing that exalts him in his own estimation, or confirms his good opinion of himself. We flatter a woman when we praise her children. A man that flattereth his neighbor, spreadeth a net for his feet. Proverbs 29:5.
To please; to gratify; as, to flatter one’s vanity or pride.
To praise falsely; to encourage by favorable notice; as, to flatter vices or crimes.
To encourage by favorable representations or indications; as, to flatter hopes. We are flattered with the prospect of peace.
flattered: Soothed by praise; pleased by commendation; gratified with hopes, false or well founded; wheedled.
flatterer: One who flatters; a fawner; a wheedler; one who praises another, with a view to please him, to gain his favor, or to accomplish some purpose. When I tell him he hates flatterers, He says he does; being then most flattered. The most abject flatterers degenerate into the greatest tyrants.
flattering: Gratifying with praise; pleasing by applause; wheedling; coaxing.
a. Pleasing to pride or vanity; gratifying to self-love; as a flattering eulogy. The minister gives a flattering account of his reception at court.
Pleasing; favorable; encouraging hope. We have a flattering prospect of an abundant harvest. The symptoms of the disease are flattering.
Practicing adulation; uttering false praise; as a flattering tongue.
flatteringly: In a flattering manner; in a manner to flatter.
Ina manner to favor; with partiality.
flattery: False praise; commendation bestowed for the purpose of gaining favor and influence, or to accomplish some purpose. Direct flattery consists in praising a person himself; indirect flattery consists in praising a person through his works or his connections. Simple pride for flattery makes demands. Just praise is only a debt, but flattery is a present.
flatulence: Windiness in the stomach; air generated in a weak stomach and intestines by imperfect digestion, occasioning distension, uneasiness, pain, and often belchings.
flatulency: Windiness in the stomach; air generated in a weak stomach and intestines by imperfect digestion, occasioning distension, uneasiness, pain, and often belchings.
flatulent: Windy; affected with air generated in the stomach and intestines.
Turgid with air; windy; as a flatulent tumor.
Generating or apt to a generate wind in the stomach. Pease are a flatulent vegetable.
Empty; vain; big without substance or reality; puffy; as a flatulent writer; flatulent vanity.
flatus: A breath; a puff of wind.
Wind generated in the stomach or other cavities of the body; flatulence.
flatwise: or adv. With the flat side downward or next to another object; not edgewise.
flaunt: To throw or spread out; to flutter; to display ostentatiously; as a flaunting show. You flaunt about the streets in your new gilt chariot. One flaunts in rags, one flutters in brocade.
To carry a pert or saucy appearance.
flavor: The quality of a substance which affects the taste or smell, in any manner. We say, the wine has a fine flavor, or a disagreeable flavor; the fruit has a bad flavor; a rose has a sweet flavor. The word then signifies the quality which is tasted or smelled; taste, odor, fragrance.
To communicate some quality to a thing, that may affect the taste or smell.
flavored: Having a quality that affects the sense of tasting or smelling; as high- flavored wine, having the quality in a high degree.
flavorless: Without flavor; tasteless; having no smell or taste.
flavorous: Pleasant to the taste or smell
flaw: A breach; a crack; a defect made by breaking or splitting; a gap or fissure; as a flaw in a scythe, knife or razor; a flaw in a china dish, or in a glass; a flaw in a wall.
A defect; a fault; any defect made by violence, or occasioned by neglect; as a flaw in reputation; a flaw in a will, or in a deed, orina statute.
A sudden burst of wind; a sudden gust or blast of short duration; a word of common use among seamen.
A sudden burst of noise and disorder; a tumult; uproar. And deluges of armies from the town . Came pouring in; I heard the mighty flaw.
flawn: A sort of custard or pie.
flawter: To scrape or pare a skin.
flawy: Full of flaws or cracks; broken; defective; faulty.
Subject to sudden gusts of wind.
flax: A plant of the genus Linum, consisting of a single slender stalk, the skin or herl of which is used for making thread and cloth, called linen, cambric, lawn, laceThe skin consists of fine fibers, which may be so separated as to be spun into threads as fine as silk.
The skin or fibrous part of the plant when broken and cleaned by hatcheling or combing.
flaxcomeb: An instrument with teeth through which flax is drawn for separating from it the tow or coarser part and the shives. In America, we call it a hatchel.
flaxdresser: One who breaks and swingles flax.
flaxplant: The Phormium, a plant in New Zealand that serves the inhabitants for flax.
flaxen: Made of flax; as flaxen thread.
Resembling flax; of the color of flax; fair, long, and flowing; as flaxen hair.
flaxy: Like flax; being of a light color; fair.
flay: To skin; to strip off the skin of an animal; as, to flay an ox.
To take off the skin or surface of any thing.
flayer: One who strips off the skin.
flea: An insect of the genus Pulex. It has two eyes and six feet; the feelers are like threads; the rostrum is inflected, setaceous, and armed with a string. The flea is remarkable for its agility, leaping to a surprising distance, and its bite is very troublesome.
fleabane: A plant of the genus Conyza.
fleabite: The bite of a flea, or the red spot caused by the bite.
A trifling wound or pain, like that of the bite of a flea.
fleabiting: The bite of a flea, or the red spot caused by the bite.
A trifling wound or pain, like that of the bite of a flea.
fleabitten: Bitten or stung by a flea.
Mean; worthless; of low birth or station.
fleam: In surgery and farriery, a sharp instrument used for opening veins for letting blood.
fleck: To spot; to streak or stripe; to variegate; to dapple. Both flecked with white, the true Arcadian strain.
flecker: To spot; to streak or stripe; to variegate; to dapple. Both flecked with white, the true Arcadian strain.
flection: The act of bending, or state of being bent.
fled: and pp. of flee; as, truth has fled.
fledge: Feathered; furnished with fethers or wings; able to fly. His locks behind, illustrious on his shoulders, fledge with wings, lay waving round.
To furnish with fethers; to supply with the fethers necessary for flight. The birds were not yet fledged enough to shift for themselves.
fledged: Furnished with fethers for flight; covered with fethers.
flee: To run with rapidity, as from danger; to attempt to escape; to hasten from danger or expected evil. The enemy fled at the first fire. Arise, take the young child and his mother, and flee into Egypt. Matthew 2:13.
To depart; to leave; to hasten away. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you. James 4:7.
fleece: The coat of wool shorn from a sheep at one time.
To shear off a covering or growth of wool./ .

To strip of money or property; to take from, by severe exactions, under color of law or justice, or pretext of necessity, or by virtue of authority. Arbitrary princes fleece their subjects; and clients complain that they are sometimes fleeced by their lawyers. This word is rarely or never used for plundering in war by a licentious soldiery; but is properly used to express a stripping by contributions levied on a conquered people.
To spread over as with wool; to make white.
fleeced: Furnished with a fleece or with fleeces; as, a sheep is well fleeced.
fleecer: One who strips or takes by severe exactions.
fleecing: Stripping of money or property by severe demands of fees, taxes or contributions.
fleecy: Covered with wool; woolly; as a fleecy flock.
Resembling wool or a fleece; soft; complicated; as fleecy snow; fleecy locks; fleecy hosiery.
fleer: To deride; to sneer; to mock; to gibe; to make a wry face in contempt, or to grin in scorn; as, to fleer and flout. Covered with an antic face, To fleer and scorn at our soleminity.
To leer; to grin with an air of civility: . A teacherous fleer on the face of deceivers.
Derision or mockery, expressed by words or looks. And mark the fleers, the gibes, and notable scorns.
Agrin of civility. A treacherous fleer on the face of deceivers.
fleering: Deriding; mocking; counterfeiting an air of civility.
fleet: in English names, denotes a flood, a creek or inlet, a bay or estuary, or a river; as in Fleet-street, North-flete, Fleet-prison.
A navy or squadron of ships; a number of ships in company, whether ships of war, or of commerce. It more generally signifies ships of war.
Swift of pace; moving or able to move with rapidity; nimble; light and quick in motion, or moving with lightness and celerity; as a fleet horse or dog.
Moving with velocity; as fleet winds.
Light; superficially fruitful; or thin; not penetrating deep; as soil.
fleetfoot: Swift of foot; running or able to run with rapidity.
fleeting: Passing rapidly; flying with velocity. 2 .a. Transient; not durable; as the fleeting hours’ or moments.
fleetness: Swiftness; rapidity; velocity; celerity; speed; as the fleetness of a horse or a deer.
fleming: A native of Flanders, or the Low Countries in Europe.
flesh: A compound substance forming a large part of an animal, consisting of the softer solids, as distinguished from the bones and the fluids. Under the general appellation of flesh, we include the muscles, fat, glands etc., which invest the bones and are covered with the skin. It is sometimes restricted to the muscles.
Animal food, in distinction from vegetable. Flesh without being qualified with acids, is too alkalescent a diet.
The body of beasts and fowls used as food, distinct from fish. In Lent, the Catholics abstain from flesh, but eat fish.
The body, as distinguished from the soul. As if this flesh, which walls about our life, Were brass impregnable.
Animal nature; animals of all kinds. The end of all flesh is come before me. Genesis 6:13.
fleshbroth: Broth made by boiling flesh in water.
fleshbrush: A brush for exciting action in the skin by friction.
fleshcolored: Being of the color of flesh.
fleshefly: A fly that feeds on flesh, and deposits her eggs in it.
fleshhook: A hook to draw flesh from a pot or caldron. 1 Samuel 2:13, 14.
fleshiness: Abundance of flesh or fat in animals; plumpness; corpulence; grossness.
fleshly: Carnal; worldly; lascivious. Abstain from fleshly lusts. 7 Peter 2:77.
Human; not celestial; not spiritual or divine. Vain of fleshly arm. Fleshly wisdom. 2 Corinthians 1:12.
fleshmeat: Animal food; the flesh of animals prepared or used for food.
fleshment: Eagerness gained by a successful initiation.
fleshmonger: One who deals in flesh; a procurer; a pimp.
fleshpot: A vessel in which flesh is cooked; hence, plenty of provisions. Exodus 16:3.
fleshy: Full of flesh; plump; musculous. The sole of his foot is fleshy.
Fat; gross; corpulent; as a fleshy man.
Full of pulp; pulpous; plump; as fruit.
fletcher: An arrow-maker; a manufacturer of bows and arrows. hence the name of Fletcher. But the use of the word as an appellative has ceased with the practice of archery.
fletz: In geology, the fletz formations, so called, consist of rocks which lie immediately over the transition rocks. These formations are so called because the rocks usually appear in beds more nearly horizontal than the transition class. These formations consist of sandstone, limestone, gypsum, calamine, chalk, coal and trap. They contain abundance of petrifactions, both of animal and vegetable origin.
flew: of fly. The people flew upon the spoil. 7 Samuel 14:32.
The large chaps of a deep-mouthed hound.
flexanimous: Having power to change the mind.
flexibility: The quality of admitting to be bent; pliancy; flexibleness; as the flexibility of rays of light.
Easiness to be persuaded; the quality of yielding to arguments, persuasion or circumstances; ductility of mind; readiness to comply; facility; as flexibility of temper.
flexible: That may be bent; capable of being turned or forced from a straight line or form without breaking; pliant; yielding to pressure; not stiff; as a flexible rod; a flexible plant.

Capable of yielding to intreaties, arguments or other moral force; that may be persuaded to compliance; not invincibly rigid; or obstinate; not inexorable. Phocion was a man of great severity, and no ways flexible to the will of the people. It often denotes, easy or too easy to yield or comply; wavering; inconstant; not firm.
Ductile; manageable; tractable; as the tender and flexible minds of youth. Flexible years or time of life, the time when the mind is tractable.
That may be turned or accommodated. This was a principle more flexible to their purpose.
flexibleness: Possibility to be bent or turned from a straight line or form without breaking; easiness to be bent; pliantness; pliancy; flexibility.
Facility of mind; readiness to comply or yield; obSequiousness; as the flexibleness of a courtier.
flexile: Pliant; pliable; easily bent; yielding to power, impulse or moral force.
flexion: A bending; a part bent; a fold.
A turn; a cast; as a flexion of the eye.
flexor: In anatomy, a muscle whose office is to bend the part to which it belongs, in opposition to the extensors.
flexuous: Winding; having turns or windings; as a flexuous rivulet.
Bending; winding; wavering; not steady; as a flexuous flame.
In botany, bending or bent; changing its direction in a curve, from joint to joint, from bud to bud, or from flower to flower.
flexure: A winding or bending; the form of bending; as the flexure of a joint .
The bending of the body; obsequious or servile cringe.
flicker: To flutter; to flap the wings without flying; to strike rapidly with the wings. And flickering on her nest made short essays to sing.
flickering: Fluttering; flapping the wings’ without flight .
a. With amorous motions of the eye. The fair Lavinia - looks a little flickering after Turnus.
flier: A part of a machine which, by moving rapidly, equalizes and regulates the motion of the whole; as the flier of a jack.
flight: The act of fleeing; the act of running away, to escape danger or expected evil; hasty departure. Pray ye that your flight be not in winter. Vatihew 24:20. To put to flight, to turn to flight, is to compel to run away; to force to escape.
The act of flying; a passing through the air by the help of wings; volation; as the flight of birds and insects.
The manner of flying. Every fowl has its particular flight; the flight of the eagle is high; the flight of the swallow is rapid, with sudden turns.
Removal from place to place by flying.
A flock of birds flying in company; as a flight of pigeons or wild geese.
flightness: The state of being flighty; wildness; slight delirium.
flight-shot: The distance which an arrow flies.
flighty: Fleeting; swift. The flighty purpose never is o’ertook.
Wild; indulging the sallies of imagination.
flimsiness: State or quality of being flimsy; thin, weak texture; weakness; want of substance or solidity.
flimsy: Weak; feeble; slight; vain; without strength or solid substance; as a flimsy pretext; a flimsy excuse; flimsy objections.
Without strength or force; spiritless. Proud of a vast extent of flimsy lines.
Thin; of loose texture; as flimsy cloth or stuff.
flinch: To shrink; to withdraw from any suffering or undertaking, from pain or danger; to fail of proceeding, or of performing any thing. Never flinch from duty. One of the parties flinched from the combat. A child, by a constant course of kindness, may be accustomed to bear very rough usage without flinching or complaining.
flinching: Failing to undertake, perform or proceed; shrinking; withdrawing.
flinder: A small piece or splinter; a fragment.
fling: To cast, send or throw from the hand; to hurl; as, to fling a stone at a bird. Tis fate that flings the dice; and as she flings, Of kings makes peasants, and of peasants.
To dart; to cast with violence; to send forth. He - like Jove, his lightning flung.
To send forth; to emit; to scatter. Every beam new transient colors flings.
To throw; to drive by violence.
flinger: One who flings; one who jeers.
flint: In natural history, a sub-species of quartz, of a yellowish or bluish gray, or grayish black color. It is amorphous, interspersed in other stones, or in nodules or rounded lumps. Its surface is generally uneven, and covered with a rind or crust, either calcarious or argillaceous. It is very hard, strikes fire with steel, and is an ingredient in glass.
A piece of the above described stone used in firearms to strike fire.
Any thing proverbially hard; as a heart of flint.
flinty: Consisting of flint; as a flinty rock. FLINT Y.
Like flint; very hard, not impressible; as a flinty heart.
Full of flint stones, as flinty ground. Flinty-slate, a mineral of two kinds, the common and the Lydian stone.
flip: A mixed liquor consisting of beer and spirit sweetened.
flipdog: An iron used, when heated, to warm flip.
flippancy: Smoothness and rapidity of speech; volubility of tongue; fluency of speech.
flippant: Of smoother, fluent and rapid speech; speaking with ease and rapidity; having a voluble tongue; talkative.
Pert; petulant; waggish. Away with flippant epilogues.
flippantly: Fluently; with ease and volubility of speech.
flippantness: fluency of speech; volubility of tongue; flippancy.
flirt: To throw with a jerk or sudden effort or exertion. The boys flirt water in each other’s faces. He flirted a glove or a handkerchief.
To toss or throw; to move suddenly; as, to flirt a fan.
To jeer or gibe; to throw harsh or sarcastic words; to utter contemptuous language, with an air of disdain.
To run and dart about; to be moving hastily from place to place; to be unsteady or fluttering. The girls flirt about the room or the street.
flirtation: A flirting; a quick sprightly motion.
flirting: Throwing; jerking; tossing; darting about; rambling and changing place hastily.
flit: To fly away with a rapid motion; to dart along; to move with celerity through the air. We say, a bird flits away, or flits in air; a cloud flits along.
To flutter; to rove on the wing.

To remove; to migrate; to pass rapidly, as a light substance, from one place to another. It became a received opinion, that the souls of men, departing this life, did flit out of one body into some other.
In Scotland, to remove from one habitation to another.
To be unstable; to easily or often moved. An the free soul to flitting air resigned.
flitch: The side of a hog salted and cured.
flittermouse: A bat; an animal that has the fur of a mouse, and membranes which answer the purpose of wings, and enable the animal to sustain itself in a fluttering flight.
flitting: A flying with lightness and celerity; a fluttering.
flixweed: The Sisymbrium sophia, a species of water-cresses, growing on walls and waste grounds.
float: That which swims or is borne on water; as a float of weeds and rushes. But particularly, a body or collection of timber, boards or planks fastened together and conveyed down a stream; a raft.
The cork or quill used on an angling line, to support it and discover the bite of a fish.
The act of flowing; flux; flood; the primary sense, but obsolete.
A quantity of earth, eighteen feet square and one deep.
floatage: Any thing that floats on the water.
float-board: A board of the water-wheel of undershot mills, which receives the impulse of the stream, by which the wheel is driven.
floating: Lying flat on the surface of the water; as a floating leaf.
floating-bridge: In the United States, a bridge, consisting of logs or timber with a floor of plank, supported wholly by the water.

In war, a kind of double bridge, the upper one projecting beyond the lower one, and capable of being moved forward by pulleys, used for carrying troops over narrow moats in attacking the outworks of a fort.
floatstone: Swimming flint, spungiform quartz, a mineral of a spungy texture, of a whitish gray color, often with a tinge of yellow. It frequently contains a nucleus of common flint.
floaty: Buoyant; swimming on the surface; light.
flocculence: The state of being in locks or flocks; adhesion in small flakes.
flocculent: Coalescing and adhering in locks or flakes. I say the liquor is broken to flocculence, when the particles of herbaceous matter, seized by those of the lime, and coalescing, appear large and flocculent.
flock: A company or collection; applied to sheep and other small animals. A flock of sheep answers to a herd of larger cattle. But the word may sometimes perhaps be applied to larger beasts, and in the plural, flocks may include all kinds of domesticated animals.

Acompany or collection of fowls of any kind, and when applied to birds on the wing, a flight; as a flock of wild-geese; a flock of ducks; a flock of blackbirds. in the United States, flocks of wild-pigeons sometimes darken the air.
A body or crowd of people. JNM .
A lock of wool or hair. Hence, a flockbed.
To gather in companies or crowds; applied to men or other animals. People flock together. They flock to the play-house. Friends daily flock.
flocking: Collecting or running together in a crowd.
flog: To beat or strike with a rod or whip; to whip; to lash; to chastise with repeated blows; a colloquial word, applied to whipping or beating for punishment; as, to flog a schoolboy or a sailor.
flogged: Whipped or scourged for punishment; chastised.
flood: A great flow of water; a body of moving water; particularly, a body of water, rising, swelling and overflowing land not usually covered with water. Thus there is a flood, every spring, in the Connecticut, which inundates the adjacent meadows. There is an annual flood in the Nile, and in the Mississippi.
The flood, by way of eminence, the deluge; the great body of water which inundated the earth in the days of Noah. Before the flood, men live to a great age.
A river; a sense chiefly poetical.
The flowing of the tide; the semi-diurnal swell or rise of water in the ocean; opposed to ebb. The ship entered the harbor on the flood. Hence flood-tide; young flood; high flood. WWV”AD .
A great quantity; an inundation; an overflowing; abundance; superabundance; as a flood of bank notes; a flood of paper currency.
floodgate: A gate to be opened for letter water flow through, or to be shut to prevent it.
An opening or passage; an avenue for a flood or great body.
flooding: Any preternatural discharge of blood from the uterus.
flood-mark: The mark or line to which the tide rises; high water mark.
flooking: In mining, an interruption or shifting of a load of ore, by a cross vein or fissure.
floor: That part of a building or room on which we walk; the bottom or lower part, consisting, in modern houses, of boards, plands or pavement; as the floor of a house, room, bar, stable or outhouse.
A platform of boards or plans laid on timbers, as in a bridge; any similar platform.
A story in a building; as the first or second floor.
A floor or earthen floor is still used in some kinds of business, made of loam, or of lime, sand and iron dust, as in malting.
The bottom of a ship, or that part which is nearly horizontal.
floored: Covered with boards, plank or pavement; furnished with a floor.
flooring: Laying a floor; furnishing with a floor.
A platform; the bottom of a room or building; pavement.
floor-timbers: The timbers on which a floor is laid.
flop: To clap or strike the wings.
To let down the brim of a hat.
flora: In antiquity, the goddess of flowers.
In modern usage, a catalogue or account of flowers or plants.
floral: Containing the flower, as a floral bud; immediately attending the flower, as a floral leaf.
Pertaining to Flora or to flowers; as floral games; floral play.
floren: An ancient gold coin of Edward III of six shillings sterling value, about 134 cents.
florence: An ancient gold coin of Edward III of six shillings sterling value, about 134 cents.
A kind of wine from Florence in Italy.
florentine: Akind of silk cloth, so called.
florescence: In botany, the season when plants expand their flowers.
floret: A little flower; the partial or separate little flower of an aggregate flower.
florid: Literally, flowery; covered or abounding with flowers; but in this sense little used.
Bright in color; flushed with red; of a lively red color; as a florid countenance; a florid cheek.
Embellished with flowers of rhetoric; enriched with lively figures; splendid; brilliant; as a florid style; florid eloquence.
floridity: Freshness or brightness of color; floridness.
floridness: Brightness or freshness of color or complexion.
Embellishment; brilliant ornaments; ambitious elegance; applied to style.
florification: The act, process or time of flowering.
florin: A coin, originally made at Florence. The name is given to different coins of gold or silver, and of different values in different countries. It is also used as a money of account.
florist: A cultivator of flowers; one skilled in flowers./\ .
One who writes a flora, or an account of plants.
floscular: In botany, a flosculous flower is a compound flower, composed entirely of florets with funnel-shaped petals, as in burdock, thistle and artichoke. This is the term used by Tournefort. For this Linne used tubulous.
flosculous: In botany, a flosculous flower is a compound flower, composed entirely of florets with funnel-shaped petals, as in burdock, thistle and artichoke. This is the term used by Tournefort. For this Linne used tubulous.
floscule: In botany, a partial or lesser floret of an aggregate flower.
flos ferri: A mineral, a variety of arragonite, called by Jameson, after Hauy, coralloidal arragonite. It occurs in little cylinders, sometimes diverging and ending in a point, and sometimes branched, like coral. Its structure is fibrous, and the surface, which is smooth, or garnished with little crystalline points, is often very white, with a silken luster. It takes this name from its being often found in cavities in veins of sparry iron.
floss: A downy or silky substance in the husks of certain plants.
flota: A fleet; but appropriately a fleet of Spanish ships which formerly sailed every year from Cadiz to Vera Crus, in Mexico, to transport to Spain the production of Spanish America.
flotage: That which floats on the sea, or on rivers.
flotilla: A little fleet, or fleet of small vessels.
flotsam: Goods lost by shipwreck, and floating on the sea. When such goods are cast on shore or found, the owner being unknown, they belong to the king.
flotson: Goods lost by shipwreck, and floating on the sea. When such goods are cast on shore or found, the owner being unknown, they belong to the king.
flounce: To throw the limbs and body one way and the other; to spring, turn or twist with sudden effort or violence; to struggle as a horse in mire. You neither fume, not fret, not flounce.
To move with jerks or agitation.
To deck with a flounce; as, to flounce a petticoat or frock.
A narrow piece of cloth sewed to a petticoat, frock or gown, with the lower border loose and spreading. The present is the age of flounces. 1827.
flounder: A flat fish of the genus Pleuronectes.
To fling the limbs and body, as in making efforts to move; to struggle as a horse in the mire; to roll, toss and tumble.
floundering: Making irregular motions; struggling with violence.
flour: The edible part of corn; meal. In the United States, the modern practice is to make a distinction between flour and meal; the word flour being more usually applied to the finer part of meal, separated from the bran, as wheat flour, rye flour. This is a just and useful distinction.
To grind and bolt; to convert into flour. Wheat used formerly to be sent to market; but now great quantities of it are floured in the interior country.
floured: Converted into flour; sprinkled with flour.
flouring: Converting into flour; sprinkling with flour.
flourish: To thrive; to grow luxuriantly; to increase and enlarge, as a healthy growing plant. The beech and the maple flourish best in a deep, rich and moist loam.
To be prosperous; to increase in wealth or honor. Bad men as frequently prosper and flourish, and that by the means of their wickedness. When all the workers of iniquity do flourish. Psalm 92:7.
To grow in grace and in good works; to abound in the consolations of religion. The righteous shall flourish like the palmtree. Psalm 92:12.
To be in a prosperous state; to grow or be augmented. We say agriculture flourishes, commerce flourishes, manufactures flourish.
flourished: flur’ished. Embellished; adorned with bold and irregular figures or lines; brandished.
flourisher: One who flourishes; one who thrives or prospers.
One who adorns with fanciful figures.
flourishing: or a. flurishing. Thriving; prosperous; increasing; making a show.
flout: To mock or insult; to treat with contempt. Phillida flouts me. He flouted us downright.
To practice mocking; to sneer; to behave with contempt. Fleer and gibe, and laugh and flout.
flouter: One who flouts and flings; a mocker.
flow: To move along an inclined plane, or on descending ground, by the operation of gravity, and with a continual change of place among the particles or parts, as a fluid. A solid body descends or moves in mass, as a ball or a wheel; but in the flowing of liquid substances, and others consisting of very fine particles, there is a constant change of the relative position of some parts of the substance, as in the case with a stream of water, of quicksilver, and of sand. Particles at the bottom and sides of the stream, being somewhat checked by friction, move slower than those in the middle and near the surface of the current. Rivers flow from springs and lakes; tears flow from the eyes.
To melt; to become liquid. That the mountains might flow down at they presence. Isaiah 64:1.
To proceed; to issue. Evils flow from different sources. Wealth flows from industry and economy. All our blessings flow from divine bounty .
To abound; to have in abundance. In that day the mountains shall drop down new wine, and the hills shall flow with milk. Joel 3:78.
To be full; to be copious; as flowing cups or goblets.
flower: In botany, that part of a plant which contains the organs of fructification, with their coverings. A flower, when complete, consists of a calyx, corol, stamen and pistil; but the essential parts are the anther and stigma, which are sufficient to constitute a flower, either together in hermaphrodite flowers, or separate in male and female flowers.

In vulgar acceptation, a blossom or flower is the flower bud of a plant, when the petals are expanded; open petals being considered as the principal thing in constituting a flower. But in botany, the petals are now considered as a finer sort of covering, and not at all necessary to constitute a flower.
The early part of life, or rather of manhood; the prime; youthful vigor; youth; as the flower of age or of life.

The best or finest part of a thing; the most valuable part. The most active and vigorous part of an army are called the flower of the troops. Young, vigorous and brave men are called the flower of a nation.
flower-de-lis: In heraldry, a bearing representing a lily, the hieroglyphic of royal majesty.
In botany, the Iris, a genus of monogynian trianders, called also flag-flower, and often written incorrectly flower-de-luce. The species are numerous.
flower-fence: The name of certain plants. The flower-fence of Barbados is of the genus Poinciana. The bastard flower-fence is the Adenanthera.
flower-garden: A garden in which flowers are chiefly cultivated.
floweriness: The state of being flowery, or of abounding with flowers.
Floridness of speech; abundance of figures.
flowering: Blossoming; blooming; expanding the petals, as plants.
Adorning with artificial flowers, or figures of blossoms.
The act of adorning with flowers.
flower-stalk: In botany, the peduncle of a plant, or the stem that supports the flower or fructification.
flowery: Full of flowers; abounding with blossoms; as a flowery field.
Adorned with artificial flowers, or the figures of blossoms.
Richly embellished with figurative language; florid; as a flowery style.
flowing: Moving as a fluid; issuing; proceeding; abounding; smooth, as style; inundating.
The act of running or moving as a fluid; an issuing; an overflowing; rise of water.
flowingness: Smoothness of diction; stream of diction.
fluke: The part of an anchor which fastens in the ground.
flown: had fled, in the following phrases, is not good English. Was reason flown. Sons of Belial, flown with insolence and wine. In the former passage, flown is used as the participle of fly or flee, both intransitive verbs, and the phrase should have been, had reason flown or fled. In the latter passage, flown is used for blown, inflated, but most improperly. Flown is the participle of the perfect or past tense of fly, but cannot regularly be used in a passive sense.
fluate: In chimistry, a salt formed by the fluoric acid combined with a base; as fluate of alumin, or of soda.
fluctuant: Moving like a wave; wavering; unsteady.
fluctuate: To move as a wave; to roll hither and thither; to wave; as a fluctuating field of air.
To float backward and forward, as on waves.
To move now in one direction and now in another; to be wavering or unsteady. Public opinion often fluctuates. Men often fluctuate between different parties and opinions. Hence,
To rise and fall; to be in an unsettled state; to experience sudden vicissitudes. The funds or the prices of stocks fluctuate with the events of the day.
fluctuating: Wavering; rolling as a wave; moving in this and that direction; rising and falling.
a. Unsteady; wavering; changeable. We have little confidence in fluctuating opinions.
A rising and falling suddenly; as fluctuations of prices or of the funds.
fluctuation: A motion like that of waves; a moving in this and that direction; as the fluctuations of the sea.
A wavering; unsteadiness; as fluctuations of opinion.
A rising and falling suddenly; as fluctuations of prices or of the funds.
fluder: An aquatic fowl of the diver kind, nearly as large as a goose.
fludder: An aquatic fowl of the diver kind, nearly as large as a goose.
flue: A passage for smoke in a chimney, leading from the fireplace to the top of the chinmey, or into another passage; as a chinmey with four flues.
Soft down or fur; very fine hair.
fluellen: The female speedwell, a plant of the genus Antirrhinum, or snapdragon.
fluency: The quality of flowing, applied to speech or language; smoothness; freedom from harshness; as fluency’ of numbers.
Readiness of utterance; facility of words; volubility; as fluency of speech; a speaker of remarkable fluency.
fluent: Flowing; passing. Motion being a fluent thing.
Ready in the use of words; voluble; copious; having words at command and uttering them with facility and smoothness; as a fluent speaker.
Flowing; voluble; smooth; as fluent speech.
fluently: With ready flow; volubly; without hesitation or obstruction; as, to speak fluently.
flugelman: In German, the leader of a file. But with us, a soldier who stands on the wing of a body of men, and marks time for the motions.
fluid: Having parts which easily move and change their relative position without separation, and which easily yield to pressure; that may flow; liquid. Water, spirit, air, are fluid substances. All bodies may be rendered fluid by heat or caloric.

Any substance whose parts easily move and change their relative position without separation, and which yields to the slightest pressure; a substance which flows, or which moves spontaneously on a plane with the least inclination; a liquid; liquor; opposed to a solid. Water, blood, chyle, are fluids.
fluidity: The quality of being capable of flowing; that quality of bodies which renders them impressible to the slightest force, and by which the parts easily move or change their relative position without a separation of the mass; a liquid state; opposed to solidity. Fluidity is the effect of heat.
fluidness: The state of being fluid; fluidity, which see.
fluke-worm: The guard-worm, a species of Fasciola.
flume: Literally, a flowing; hence, the passage or channel for the water that drives a mill-wheel.
flummery: A sort of jelly made of flour or meal; pap. Milk and flummery are very fit for children.
In vulgar use, any thing insipid or nothing to the purpose; flattery.
flung: and pp. of fling. Several statues the Romans themselves flung into the river.
fluoborate: A compound of fluoboric acid with a base.
fluoboric: The fluoboric acid or gas is a compound of fluorine and boron.
fluor: In mineralogy, fluate of lime. Fluor spar is the foliated fluate of lime. This mineral, though sometimes massive, is almost always regularly crystallized. Its crystals present most frequently the form of a cube, often perfect, sometimes truncated on all its edges by planes, which form with the sides of the cube an angle of degrees. The colors are very numerous and beautiful. The flutate of lime, fluor, was so named from its use as a flux for certain ores.
fluoric: Pertaining to fluor; obtained from fluor; as fluoric acid.
fluorin: The supposed basis of fluoric acid.
fluorine: The supposed basis of fluoric acid.
fluorous: The fluorous acid is the acid of fluor in its first degree of oxygenation.
fluosilicate: In chiminstry, a compound of fluoric acid, containing silex, with some other substance.
fluosilicic: Composed of or containing fluoric acid with silex.
flurry: A sudden blast or gust, or a light temporary breeze; as a flurry of wind. It is never with us applied to a storm of duration.
A sudden shower of short duration; as a flurry of snow.
To put in agitation; to excite or alarm.
flush: To flow and spread suddenly; to rush; as, blood flushes into the face.
To come in haste; to start.
To appear suddenly, as redness or a blush. A blush rose on their cheeks, flushing and fading like the changeful play of colors on a dolphin.
To become suddenly red; to glow; as, the cheeks flush.
To be gay, splendid or beautiful. At once, arrayed in all the colors of the flushing year, the garden glows.
flushed: Overspread or tinged with a red color from the flowing of blood to the face. We say, the skin, face or cheek is flushed.
Elated; excited; animated; as flushed with joy or success.
flushing: A glow of red in the face.
fluster: To make hot and rosy, as with drinking; to heat; to hurry; to agitate; to confuse.
To be in a heat or bustle; to be agitated.
flute: Asmall wind instrument; a pipe with lateral holes or stops, played by blowing with the mouth, and by stopping and opening the holes with the fingers.

A channel in a column or pillar; a perpendicular furrow or cavity, cut along the shaft of a column or pilaster; so called from its resemblance to a flute. It is used chiefly in the lonic order; sometimes in the Composite and Corinthian; rarely in the Doric and Tuscan. It is called also a reed.

A long vessel or boat, with flat ribs or floor timbers, round behind, and swelled in the middle; a different orthography of float. Armed in flute. An armed ship, with her guns of the lower tier and part of those of the upper tier removed, used as a transport, is said to be armed in flute.
To form flutes or channels in a column.
fluted: In music, thin; fine; flutelike; as fluted notes.
fluting: Channeling; cutting furrows; as in a column.
A channel or furrow in a column; fluted work.
flutter: To move or flap the wings rapidly, without flying, or with short flights; to hover. As an eagle stirreth up her next, fluttereth over her young, spreadeth abroad her wings - Deuteronomy 32:11.
To move about briskly, irregularly or with great bustle and show, without consequence. No rag, no scrap of all the beau or wit, that once so fluttered, and that once so writ.
To move with quick vibrations or undulations; as a fluttering fan; a fluttering sail.
To be in agitation; to move irregularly; to fluctuate; to be in uncertainty. How long we fluttered on the wings of doubtful success. His thoughts are very fluttering and wandering.
fluttering: Flapping the wings without flight or with short flights; hovering; fluctuating; agitating; throwing into confusion.
The act of hovering, or flapping the wings without flight; a wavering agitation.
fluviatic: Belonging to rivers; growing or living in streams or ponds; as a fluviatic plant.
fluvial: Belonging to rivers; growing or living in streams or ponds; as a fluviatic plant.
flux: The act of flowing; the motion or passing of a fluid.
The moving or passing of any thing in continued succession. Things in this life, are in a continual flux.
Any flow or issue of matter. In medicine, an extraordinary issue or evacuation from the bowels or other part; as the bloody flux or dysentery, hepatic flux.
In hydrography, the flow of the tide. The ebb is called reflux.

In metallurgy, any substance or mixture used to promote the fusion of metals or minerals, as alkalies, borax, tartar and other saline matter, or in large operations limestone or fluor. Alkaline fluxes are either the crude, the white or the black flux.
fluxation: A flowing or passing away, and giving place to others.
fluxed: Melted; fused; reduced to a flowing state.
fluxible: Capable of being melted or fused, as a mineral.
fluxility: The quality of admitting fusion; possibility of being fused or liquified.
fluxion: Fluxions, in mathematics, the analysis of infinitely small variable quantities, or a method of finding an infinitely small quantity, which being taken an infinite number of times, becomes equal to a quantity given. In fluxions, magnitudes are supposed to be generated by motion; a line by the motion of a point, a surface by the motion of a line, and a solid by the motion of a surface. And some part of a figure is supposed to be generated by a uniform motion, in consequence of which the other parts may increase uniformly, or with an accelerated or retarded motion, or may decrease in any of these ways, and the computations are made by tracing the comparative velocities with which the parts flow. A fluxion is an infinitely small quantity, an increment; the infinitely small increase of the fluent or flowing quantity.
fly: To move through air by the aid of wings, as fowls.
To pass or move in air, by the force of wind or other impulse; as, clouds and vapors fly before the wind. A ball flies from a cannon, an arrow from a bow.
To rise in air, as light substances, by means of a current of air or by having less specific gravity than air, as smoke. Man is born to trouble, as the sparks fly upward. Job 5:7.
To move or pass with velocity or celerity, either on land or water. He flew to the relief of his distressed friend. The ship flies upon the main.
To move rapidly, in any manner; as, a top flies about.
flybane: A plant called catch-fly, of the genus Silene.
flybitten: Marked by the bite of flies.
flyblow: To deposit an egg in any thing, as a fly; to taint with the eggs which produce maggots. Like a flyblown cake of tallow.
flyboat: A large flat-bottomed Dutch vessel, whose burden is from 600 to 1200 tons, with a stern remarkably high, resembling a Gothic turret, and very broad buttocks below.
flycatcher: In zoology, a genus of birds, the Muscicapa, with a bill flatted at the base, almost triangular, notched at the upper mandible, and beset with bristles. These birds are of the order of Passers, and the species are very numerous.
flyer: One that flies or flees; usually written flier.

In architecture, stairs that do not wind, but are made of an oblong square figure, and whose fore and back sides are parallel to each other, and so are their ends. The second of these flyers stands parallel behind the first, the third behind the second, and so are said to fly off from one another.
A performer in Mexico, who flies round an elevated post.
flyfish: To angle with flies for bait.
flyfishing: Angling; the art or practice of angling for fish with flies, natural or artificial, for bait.
fly-honeysuckle: A plant, the Lonicera. The African fly-honeysuckle is the Halleria.
flying: Moving in air by means of wings; passing rapidly; springing; bursting; avoiding.
a. Floating; waving; as flying colors. 3 .a. Moving; light, and suited for prompt motion; as a flying camp. Flying colors, a phrase expressing triumph.
flying-bridge: A bridge of pontoons; also, a bridge composed of two boats.
flying-fish: A small fish which flies by means of its pectoral fins. It is of the genus Exocoetus.
flying-party: In military affairs, a detachment of men employed to hover about an enemy.
flying-pinion: The part of a clock, having a fly or fan, by which it fathers air, and checks the rapidity of the clock’s motion, when the weight descends in the striking part.
flytrap: In botany, a species of sensitive plant, called Venus’ Fly-trap, the Dionaea Muscipula; a plant that has the power of seizing insects that light on it.
flytree: A tree whose leaves are said to produce flies, from a little bag on the surface.
foal: To bring forth a colt or filly; to bring forth young as a mare or a she-ass.
The young of the equine genus of quadrupeds and of either sex; a colt; a filly .
To bring forth young, as a mare and certain other beasts.
foam: Froth; spume; the substance which is formed on the surface of liquors by fermentation or violent agitation, consisting of bubbles.
To froth; to gather foam. The billows foam. A horse foams at the mouth when violently heated.
To be in a rage; to be violently agitated. He foameth and gnasheth with his teeth. Mark 9:18.
To throw out with rage or violence; with out. Foaming out their own shame. Jude 13.
foamy: Covered with foam; frothy. Behold how high the foamy billows ride!
fob: A little pocket for a watch.
To cheat; to trick; to impose on. To fob off, to shift off by an artifice; to put aside; to delude with a trick.
focal: Belonging to a focus; as a focal point; focal distance.
focil: The greater focil is the ulna or tibia, the greater bone of the fore-arm or leg. The lesser focil is the radius or fibula, the lesser bone of the fore-arm or leg.
focus: In optics, a point in which any number of rays of light meet, after being reflected or refracted; as the focus of a lens.

In geometry and conic sections, a certain point in the parabola, ellipsis and hyperbola, where rays reflected from all parts of these curves, concur or meet. The focus of an ellipsis, is a point towards each end of the longer axis, from which two right lines drawn to any point in the circumference, shall together be equal to the longer axis. The focus of a parabola, is a point in the axis within the figure, and distant from the vertex by the fourth part of the parameter. The focus of a hyperbola, is a point in the principal axis, within the opposite hyperbolas, from which if any two lines are drawn, meeting in either of the opposite hyperbolas, the difference will be equal to the principal axis.
A central point; point of concentration.
fodder: Food or dry food for cattle, horses and sheep, as hay, straw and other kinds of vegetables. The word is never applied to pasture.
In mining, a measure containing 20 hundred, or 22 1/ hundred.
To feed with dry food, or cut grass; to furnish with hay, straw, oatsFarmers fodder their cattle twice or thrice in a day.
foddered: Fed with dry food, or cut grass; as, to fodder cows.
fodient: Digging; throwing up with a spade.
foe: An enemy; one who entertains personal enmity, hatred, grudge or malice against another. A man’s foes shall be they of his own household. Matthew 10:36.

An enemy in war; one of a nation at war with another, whether he entertains enmity against the opposing nation or not; an adversary. Either three years famine, or three months to be destroyed before they foes. 7 Chronicles 21:12.
Foe, like enemy, in the singular, is used to denote an opposing army, or nation at war.
An opponent; an enemy; one who opposes any thing in principle; an ill-wisher; as a foe to religion; a foe to virtue; a foe to the measures of the administration.
To treat as an enemy. Obs.
fog: A dense watery vapor, exhaled from the earth, or from rivers and lakes, or generated in the atmosphere near the earth. it differs from mist, which is rain in very small drops.
A cloud of dust or smoke.
After-grass; a second growth of grass; but it signifies also long grass that remains on land. Dead grass, remaining on land during winter, is called in New England, the old tore.
fogbank: At sea, an appearance in hazy weather sometimes resembling land at a distance, but which vanishes as it is approached.
foggage: Rank grass not consumed or mowed in summer.
fogginess: The state of being foggy; a state of the air filled with watery exhalations.
foggy: Filled or abounding with fog or watery exhalations; as a foggy atmosphere; a foggy morning.
Cloudy; misty; damp with humid vapors.
Producing frequent fogs; as a foggy climate.
foh: an exclamation of abhorrence or contempt, the same as poh and fy.
foible: A particular moral weakness; a failing. When we speak of a man’s foible, in the singular, which is also called his weak side, we refer to a predominant failing. We use also the plural, foibles, to denote moral failings or defects. It is wise in every man to know his own foibles.
foil: To frustrate; to defeat; to render vain or nugatory, as an effort or attempt. The enemy attempted to pass the river, but was foiled. He foiled his adversaries. And by a mortal man at length am foiled.
To blunt; to dull. When light wing’d toys of feathered cupid foil - .
To defeat; to interrupt, or to render imperceptible; as, to foil the scent in a chase.
Defeat; frustration; the failure of success when on the point of being secured; miscarriage. Death never won a stake with greater toil, nor e’er was fate to near a foil.
A blunt sword, or one that has a button at the end covered with leather; used in fencing. Isocrates contended with a foil, against Demosthenes with a sword.
foiler: One who frustrates another, and gains an advantage himself.
foiling: Among hunters, the slight mark of a passing deer on the grass.
foist: To insert surreptitiously, wrongfully, or without warrant. Lest negligence or partiality might admit or foist in abuses and corruption.
A light and fast sailing ship. Obs.
fold: A pen or inclosure for sheep; a place where a flock of sheep is kept, whether in the field or under shelter.
A flock of sheep. Hence in a scriptural sense, the church, the flock of the Shepherd of Israel. Other sheep I have, which are not of this fold. John 10:16.
The doubling of any flexible substance, as cloth; complication; a plait; one part turned or bent and laid on another; as a fold of linen.
In composition, the same quantity added; as two fold, four fold, ten fold, that is, twice as much, four times as much, ten times as much.
folded: Doubled; laid in plaits; complicated; kept in a fold.
folder: An instrument used in folding paper.
folding: Doubling; laying in plaits; keeping in a fold. 2.a. Doubling; that may close over another, or that consists of leaves which may close one over another; as a folding door.
Among farmers, the keeping of sheep in inclosures on arable land.
foliaceous: Leafy; having leaves intermixed with flowers; as a foliaceous spike. Foliaceous glands are those situated on leaves.
Consisting of leaves or thin lamins; having the form of a leaf or plate; as foliaceous spar.
foliage: Leaves in general; as a tree of beautiful foliage.
A cluster of leaves, flowers and branches; particularly, the representation of leaves, flowers and branches, in architecture, intended to ornament and enrich capitals, friezes, pediments.
To work or to form into the representation of leaves.
foliate: To beat into a leaf, or thin plate or lamin.
To spread over with a thin coat of tin and quicksilver; as, to foliate a looking-glass.
In botany, leafy; furnished with leaves; as a foliate stalk.
foliated: Spread or covered with a thin plate or foil.

In mineralogy, consisting of plates; resembling or in the form of a plate; lamellar; as a foliated fracture. Minerals that consist of grains, and are at the same time foliated, are called granularly foliated.
foliating: Covering with a leaf or foil.
foliation: In botany, the leafing of plants; vernation; the disposition of the nascent leaves within the bud.
The act of beating a metal into a thin plate, leaf or foil .
The act or operation of spreading foil over the back side of a mirror or looking-glass.
foliature: The state of being beaten into foil.
folio: A book of the largest size, formed by once doubling a sheet of paper.
Among merchants, a page, or rather both the right and left hand pages of an account book, expressed by the same figure.
foliole: A leaflet; one of the single leaves, which together constitute a compound leaf.
foliomort: Of a dark yellow color, or that of a faded leaf; filemot.
folious: In botany, having leaves intermixed with the flowers.
folk: People in general, or any part of them without distinction. What do folks say respecting the war? Men love to talk about the affairs of other folks.
Certain people, discriminated from others; as old folks, and young folks. Children sometimes call their parents, the old folks. So we say sick folks; poor folks; proud folks.
In scripture, the singular number is used; as a few sick folk; impotent folk. Mark 6:5; John 5:3.
Animals. The coneys are but a feeble folk. Proverbs 30:26.
folkland: In English law, copyhold land; land held by the common people, at the will of the lord.
folkmote: An assembly of the people, or of bishops, thanes, aldermen and freemen, to consult respecting public affairs; an annual convention of the people, answering in some measure, to a modern parliament; a word used in England before the Norman conquest, after which, the national Council was called a parliament. But some authors allege that the folkmote was an inferior meeting or court.
follicle: In botany, a univalvular pericarp; a seed vessel opening on one side longitudinally, and having the seeds loose in it.
An air bag; a vessel distended with air; as at the root in Utricularia, and on the leaves in Aldrovanda.
A little bag, in animal bodies; a gland; a folding; a cavity.
follow: To go after or behind; to walk, ride or move behind, but in the same direction. Soldiers will usually follow a brave officer.
To pursue; to chase; as an enemy, or as game.
To accompany; to attend in a journey. And Rebekah arose, and her damsels, and they rode on the camels, and followed the man. Genesis 24:61.
To accompany; to be of the same company; to attend, for any purpose. Luke 5:27, 28.
To succeed in order of time; to come after; as a storm is followed by acalm. Signs following signs lead on the mighty year.
followed: Pursued; succeeded; accompanied; attended; imitated; obeyed; observed; practiced; adhered to.
follower: One who comes, goes or moves after another, in the same course.

One that takes another as his guide in doctrines, opinions or example; one who receives the opinions, and imitates the example of another; an adherent; an imitator. That ye be not slothful, but followers of them who, through faith and patience, inherit the promises. Hebrews 6:12.
One who obeys, worships and honors. Be ye followers of God, as dear children. Ephesians 5:1.
An adherent; a disciple; one who embraces the same system; as a follower of Plato.
An attendant; a companion; an associate or a dependent. The warrior distributed the plunder among his followers. No follower, but a friend.
following: Coming or going after or behind; pursuing; attending; imitating; succeeding in time; resulting from as an effect or an inference; adhering to; obeying, observing; using, practicing; proceeding in the same course.
folly: Weakness of intellect; imbecility of mind. want of understanding. A fool layeth open his folly. Proverbs 13:16.

A weak or absurd act not highly criminal; an act which is inconsistent with the dictates of reason, or with the ordinary rules of prudence. In this sense it may be used in the singular, but is generally in the plural. Hence we speak of the follies of youth.

An absurd act which is highly sinful; any conduct contrary to the laws of God or man; sin; scandalous crimes; that which violates moral precepts and dishonors the offender. Shechem wrought folly in Israel. Achan wrought folly in Israel. Genesis 34:7; Joshua 7:15.
fomahant: A star of the first magnitude, in the constellation Aquarius.
foment: To apply warm lotions to; to bathe with warm medicated liquors, or with flannel dipped in warm water.
To cherish with heat; to encourage growth.
To encourage; to abet; to cherish and promote by excitements; in a bad sense; as, to foment ill humors. So we say, to foment troubles or disturbances; to foment intestine broils.
fomentation: The act of applying warm liquors to a part of the body, by means of flannels dipped in hot water or medicated decoctions, for the purpose of easing pain, by relaxing the skin, or of discussing tumors.
The lotion applied, or to be applied to a diseased part.
fomenter: One who foments; one who encourages or instigates; as a fomenter of sedition.
fond: Foolish; silly; weak; indiscreet; imprudent; . Grant I may never prove so fond . To trust man on his oath or bond. Fond thoughts may fall into some idle brain.
Foolishly tender and loving; doting; weakly indulgent; as a fond mother or wife.
Much pleased; loving ardently; delighted with. A child is fond of play; a gentleman is fond of his sports, or of his country seat. In present usage, fond does not always imply weakness or folly.
Relishing highly. The epicure is fond of high seasoned food. Multitudes of men are too fond of strong drink.
fondle: To treat with tenderness; to caress; as, a nurse fondles a child.
fondling: A person or thing fondled or caressed.
fondly: Foolishly; weakly; imprudently; with indiscreet affection. Fondly we think we merit honor then, When we but praise ourselves in other men.
With great or extreme affection. We fondly embrace those who are dear to us.
