fondness: Foolishness; weakness; want of sense or judgment. Obs.
Tender passion; warm affection. Her fondness for a certain earl began when I was but a girl.
Strong inclination or propensity; as a fondness for vice or sin.
Strong appetite or relish; as fondness for ardent spirit, or for a particular kind of food.
font: A large basin or stone vessel in which water is contained for baptizing children or other persons in the church.

A complete assortment of printing types of one size, including a due proportion of all the letters in the alphabet, large and small, points, accents, and whatever else is necessary for printing with that letter.
fontal: Pertaining to a fount, fountain, source or origin.
fontanel: An issue for the discharge of humors from the body.
A vacancy in the infant cranium, between the frontal and parietal bones, and also between the parietal and occipital, at the two extremities of the sagittal suture.
fontange: fontanj’. A knot of ribbons on the top of a head-dress.
food: In a general sense, whatever is eaten by animals for nourishment, and whatever supplies nutriment to plants.
Meat; aliment; flesh or vegetables eaten for sustaining human life; victuals; provisions; whatever is or may be eaten for nourishment. Feed me with food convenient for me. Proverbs 30:8.
Whatever supplies nourishment and growth to plants, as water, carbonic acid gasManuring substances furnish plants with food.
Something that sustains, nourishes and augments. Flattery is the food of vanity: .
foodless: Without food; destitute of provisions; barren.
fool: One who is destitute of reason, or the common powers of understanding; an idiot. Some persons are born fools, and are called natural fools; others may become fools by some injury done to the brain.

In common language, a person who is somewhat deficient in intellect, but not an idiot; or a person who acts absurdly; one who does not exercise his reason; one who pursues a course contrary to the dictates of wisdom. Experience keeps a dear school, but fools will learn in no other.

In scripture, fool is often used for a wicked or depraved person; one who acts contrary to sound wisdom in his moral deportment; one who follows his own inclinations, who prefers trifling and temporary pleasures to the service of God and eternal happiness. The fool hath said in his heart, there is no God. Psalm 14:7.

A weak christian; a godly person who has much remaining sin and unbelief. O fools, and slow of heart to believe all the prophets have written. Luke 24:25. Also, one who is accounted or called a food by ungodly men. Corinthians 4:10.
A term of indignity and reproach. To be thought knowing, you must first put the fool upon all mankind.
foolery: The practice of folly; habitual folly; attention to trifles.
An act of folly or weakness.
foolhardiness: Courage without sense or judgment; mad rashness.
foolhardy: Daring without judgment; madly rash and adventurous; foolishly bold.
foolish: Void of understanding or sound judgment; weak in intellect; applied to general character.
Unwise; imprudent; acting without judgment or discretion in particular things.
Proceeding from folly, or marked with folly; silly; vain; trifling. But foolish questions avoid. 2 Timothy 2:23.
Ridiculous; despicable. A foolish figure he must make.
In scripture, wicked; sinful; acting without regard to the divine law and glory, or to one’s own eternal happiness. O foolish Galatians - Galatians 3:7.
foolishly: Weakly; without understanding or judgment; unwisely; indiscreetly.
Wickedly; sinfully; . I have done very foolishly. 2 Samuel 24:10.
foolishness: Foolish practice; want of wisdom or good judgment.
In a scriptural sense, absurdity; folly. The preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness. Corinthians 1:18.
foolscap: A kind of paper of small size.
fooltrap: A trap to catch fools; as a fly trap.
foot: In animal bodies, the lower extremity of the leg; the part of the leg which treads the earth in standing or walking, and by which the animal is sustained and enabled to step.
That which bears some resemblance to an animal’s foot in shape or office; the lower end of any thing that supports a body; as the foot of a table.
The lower part; the base; as the foot of a column or of a mountain.
The lower part; the bottom; as the foot of an account; the foot of a sail.
football: A ball consisting of an inflated bladder, cased in leather, to be driven by the foot.
The sport or practice of kicking the football.
footboy: A menial; an attendant in livery.
footbreadth: The breadth of the foot. Deuteronomy 2:5.
footbridge: A narrow bridge for foot passengers.
footed: Kicked; trod; summed up; furnished with a foot, as a stocking.
Shaped in the foot; as footed like a goat.
footfight: A conflict by persons on foot, in opposition to a fight on horseback.
foothalt: A disease incident to sheep, and said to proceed from a worm, which enters between the claws.
foothold: That which sustains the feet firmly and prevents them from slipping of moving; that on which one may tread or rest securely.
foothot: Immediately; a word borrowed from hunting.
footing: Dancing; treading; settling; adding a new foot.
Ground for the foot; that which sustains; firm foundation to stand on . In ascents, every step gained is a footing and help to the next.
footlicker: A mean flatterer; a sycophant; a fawner.
footman: A soldier who marches and fights on foot.
A menial servant; a runner; a servant in livery.
footmanship: The art or faculty of a runner.
footmantle: A garment to keep the gown clean in riding.
footpace: A slow step, as in walking; a broad stair.
footpad: A highwayman or robber on foot.
footpath: A narrow path or way for foot passengers only.
footpost: A post or messenger that travels on foot.
footrope: The lower boltrope, to which the lower edge of a sail is sewed. Also, a horse or rope to support men when reefing
footrot: An ulcer in the feet of sheep.
footsoldier: A soldier that serves on foot.
footstalk: In botany, a petiole; a partial stem supporting the leaf, or connecting it with the stem or branch. Sometimes, but rarely, the same footstalk supports both the leaf and fructification, as in Turnera and Hibiscus.
footstep: A track; the mark or impression of the foot.
Token; mark; visible sign of a course pursued; as the footsteps of divine wisdom.
Footsteps, plural, example; as, follow the footsteps of good men.
footstool: A stool for the feet; that which supports the feet of one when sitting. To make enemies a footstool, is to reduce them to entire subjection. Psalm 110:1.
footwaling: The whole inside planks or lining of a ship.
fop: A vain man of weak understanding and much ostentation; one whose ambition is to gain admiration by showy dress and pertness; a gay trifling man; a coxcomb.
foppery: Affectation of show or importance; showy folly; as the foppery of dress or of manners.
Folly; impertinence. Let not the sound of shallow foppery enter my sober house.
Foolery; vain or idle practice; idle affectation.
foppish: Vain of dress; making an ostentatious display of gay clothing; dressing in the extreme of fashion.
foppishly: With vain ostentation of dress; in a trifling or affected manner.
foppishness: Vanity and extravagance in dress; showy vanity.
for: Against; in the place of; as a substitute or equivalent, noting equal value or satisfactory compensation, either in barter and sale, in contract, or in punishment. “And Joseph gave them bread in exchange for horses, and for flocks, and for the cattle of the herds;” that is, according to the original, he gave them bread against horses like the Gr. Genesis 48:17. Buy us and our land for bread. Genesis 47:19. And if any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot. Exodus 21:23,

In the place of; instead of; noting substitution of persons, or agency of one in the place of another with equivalent authority. An attorney is empowered to act for his principal. Will you take a letter and deliver it for me at the post office? that is, in my place, or for my benefit.
In exchange of; noting one thing taken or given in place of another; as, to quit the profession of law for that of a clergyman.N .
In the place of; instead of; as, to translate a poem line for line.

In the character of; noting resemblance; a sense derived from substitution or standing in the place of, like in the Greek. If a man can be fully assured of any thing for a truth, without having examined, what is there that he may not embrace for truth? But let her go for an ungrateful woman. I hear for certain, and do speak the truth. He quivered with his feet and lay for dead.
forage: Food of any kind for horses and cattle, as grass; pasture, hay, corn and oats.
The act of providing forage. Col. Mawhood completed his forage unmolested. If the forage is to be made at a distance from the camp - .
Search for provisions; the act of feeding abroad.
To collect food for horses and cattle, by wandering about and feeding or stripping the country.
To wander far; to rove. Obs.
forager: One that goes in search of food for horses or cattle.
foraging: or a. Collecting provisions for horses and cattle, or wandering in search of food; ravaging; stripping. The general sent out a foraging party, with a guard.
An inroad or incursion for forage or plunder.
foraminous: Full of holes; perforated in many places; porous.
forbear: To stop; to cease; to hold from proceeding; as, forbear to repeat these reproachful words.
To pause; to delay; as, forbear a while.
To abstain; to omit; to hold one’s self from motion or entering on an affair. Shall I go against Ramoth Gilead to battle, or shall I forbear? Kings 22:6.
To refuse; to decline. Whether they will hear, or whether they will forbear. Ezekiel 2:5,
forbearance: The act of avoiding, shunning or omitting; either the cessation or intermission of an act commenced, or a withholding from beginning an act. Liberty is the power of doing or forbearing an action, according as the doing or forbearance has a preference in the mind. The forbearance of sin is followed with satisfaction of mind.
Command of temper; restraint of passions.

The exercise of patience; long suffering; indulgence towards those who injure us; lenity; delay of resentment or punishment. Or despisest thou the riches of his goodness, and forbearance, and long suffering? Romans 2:4.
forbearing: Ceasing; pausing; withholding from action; exercising patience and indulgence.
A ceasing or restraining from action; patience; long suffering.
forbid: pret. forbad; pp. forbidLiterally, to bid or command against. Hence,

To prohibit; to interdict; to command to forbear or not to do. The laws of God forbid us to swear. Good manners also forbid us to use profane language. All servile labor and idle amusements on the sabbath are forbidden.
To command not to enter; as, I have forbid him my house or presence. This phrase seems to be elliptical; to forbid from entering or approaching.
To oppose; to hinder; to obstruct. An impassable river forbids the approach of the army. A blaze of glory that forbids the sight.
forbiddance: Prohibition; command or edict against a thing.
forbidder: He or that which forbids or enacts a prohibition.
forbidding: a. Repelling approach; repulsive; raising abhorrence, aversion or dislike; disagreeable; as a forbidding aspect; a forbidding formality; a forbidding air.
forborne: of forbear. Few ever repented of having forborne to speak.
force: Strength; active power; vigor; might; energy that may be exerted; that physical property in a body which may produce action or motion in another body, or may counteract such motion. By the force of the muscles we raise a weight, or resist an assault.
Momentum; the quantity of power produced by motion or the action of one body on another; as the force of a cannon ball.
That which causes an operation or moral effect; strength; energy; as the force of the mind, will or understanding.
Violence; power exerted against will or consent; compulsory power. Let conquerors consider that force alone can keep what force as obtained.
Strength; moral power to convince the mind. There is great force in an argument.
forced: Compelled; impelled; driven by violence; urged; stormed; ravished. 2.a. Affected; overstrained; unnatural; as a forced style.
forcedness: The state of being forced; distortion.
forceful: Impelled by violence; driven with force; acting with power. Against the steed he threw his forceful spear.
forceless: Having little or not force; feeble; impotent.
forcemeat: A kind of stuffing in cookery.
forceps: Literally, a pair of pinchers or tongs. In surgery, an instrument for extracting any thing from a wound, and for like purposes. A pair of scissors for cutting off or dividing the fleshy membranous parts of the body.
forcer: He or that which forces, drives or constrains.
The embolus of a pump; the instrument by which water is driven up a pump.
forcible: Powerful; strong; mighty; as a punishment forcible to bridle sin.
Violent; impetuous; driving forward with force; as a forcible stream.
Efficacious; active; powerful. Sweet smells are most forcible in dry substances when broken.
Powerful; acting with force; impressive; as forcible words or arguments.
Containing force; acting by violence; as forcible means.
forcibly: Strongly; powerfully; with power or energy; impressively. The gospel offers such considerations as are fit to work very forcibly on our hopes and fears.
Impetuously; violently; with great strength; as a stream rushing forcibly down a precipice.
forcing: Causing to ripen before the natural season, as fruit; or causing to produce ripe fruit prematurely, as a tree.
Fining wine by a speedy process.
In gardening, the art of raising plants, flowers, and fruits, at an earlier season than the natural one, by artificial heat.
The operation of fining wines by a speedy process.
forcipated: Formed like a pair of pinchers to open and inclose; as a forcipated mouth.
ford: A place in a river or other water, where it may be passed by man or beast on foot, or by wading.
A stream; a current. Permit my ghost to pass the Stygian ford.
To pass or cross a river or other water by treading or walking on the bottom; to pass through water by wading; to wade through.
fordable: That may be waded or passed through on foot, as water.
fording: Wading; passing through on foot as water.
fordo: To destroy; to undo; to ruin; to weary.
fore: Properly, advanced, or being in advance of something in motion or progression; as the fore end of a chain carried in measuring land; the fore oxen or horses in a team.
Advanced in time; coming in advance of something; coming first; anterior; preceding; prior; as the fore part of the last century; the fore part of the day, week or year.
Advanced in order or series; antecedent; as the fore part of a writing or bill: .
Being in front or towards the face; opposed to back or behind; as the fore part of a garment.
Going first; usually preceding the other part; as the fore part of a ship, or of a coach.
foreadmonish: To admonish beforehand, or before the act or event.
foreadvise: s as z To advise or counsel before the time of action or before the event; to preadmonish.
forealledge: foreallej’. To alledge or cite before.
foreappoint: To set, order or appoint beforehand.
forearm: To arm or prepare for attack or resistance before the time of need.
forebode: To foreknow; to be prescient of; to feel a secret sense of something future; as, my heart forebodes a sad reverse.
foreboder: One who forebodes; a prognosticator; a soothsayer.
forebrace: A rope applied to the fore yard-arm to change the position of the foresail.
foreby: Near; hard by; fast by. Obs.
forecast: To foresee; to provide against. It is wisdom to forecast consequences.
To scheme; to plan before execution. He shall forecast his devices against the strong holds. Daniel 11:24.
To adjust; contrive or appoint beforehand . The time so well forecast.
To form a scheme previously; to contrive beforehand. Forecasting how his foe he might annoy.
Previous contrivance; foresight, or the antecedent determination proceeding from it; as a man of little forecast.
forecaster: One who foresees or contrives beforehand.
forecastle: A short deck in the forepart of a ship above the upper deck usually terminated in ships of war with a breast-work; the foremost part forming the top of the beak-head, and the hind part reaching to the after part of the fore chains.
forecited: Cited or quoted before or above.
foreclose: s as z. To shut up; to preclude; to stop; to prevent. The embargo with Spain foreclosed this trade. To foreclose a mortgager, in law, is to cut him off from his equity of redemption, or the power of redeeming the mortgaged premises, by a judgment of court.
foreclosure: The act of foreclosing, or depriving a mortgager of the right of redeeming a mortgaged estate.
foredate: To date before the true time.
foredeck: The forepart of a deck, or of a ship.
foredesign: To plan beforehand; to intend previously.
foredoor: The door in the front of a house.
fore-end: The end which precedes; the anterior part.
forefather: An ancestor; one who precedes another in the line of genealogy, in any degree; usually in a remote degree.
forefend: To hinder; to fend off; to avert; to prevent approach; to forbid or prohibit.

To defend; to guard; to secure. This word, like the L. arceo, is applied to the thing assailing, and to the thing assailed. To drive back or resist that which assails, is to hinder its approach, to forbid or avert, and this act defends the thing threatened or assailed.
forefinger: The finger next to the thumb; the index; called by our Saxon ancestors, the shoot-finger, from its use in archery.
forefoot: One of the anterior feet of a quadruped or multiped.
In a ship, a piece of timber which terminates the keel at the fore- end.
forefront: The foremost part. The forefront of the battle, is the part where the contest is most warm, and where a soldier is most exposed. 2 Samuel 11:15.
forego: To forbear to possess or enjoy; voluntarily to avoid the enjoyment of good. Let us forego the pleasures of sense, to secure immortal bliss.
To give up; to renounce; to resign. But this word is usually applied to things not possessed or enjoyed, and which cannot be resigned.
To go before; to precede. Obs.
foregoing: Forbearing to have, possess or enjoy. 2.a. Preceding; going before, in time or place; antecedent; as a foregoing period of time; a foregoing clause in a writing.
foregone: Forborne to be possessed or enjoyed.
foreground: The part of the field or expanse of a picture which seems to lie before the figures.
forehand: The part of a horse which is before the rider.
Done sooner than is regular. And so extenuate the forehand sin.
forehanded: Early; timely; seasonable; as a forehanded care.
In America, in good circumstances as to property; free from debt and possessed of property; as a forehanded farmer.
Formed in the foreparts. A substantial true-bred beast, bravely forehanded.
forehead: The part of the face which extends from the hair on the top of the head to the eyes.
forehew: To hew or cut in front.
forehook: In ships, a breast-hook; a piece of timber placed across the stem to unite the bows and strengthen the forepart of the ship.
forehorse: The horse in a team which goes foremost.
foreign: Belonging to another nation or country; alien; not of the country in which one resides; extraneous. We call every country foreign, which is not within the jurisdiction of our own government. In this sense, Scotland before the union was foreign to England, and Canada is now foreign to the United States. More generally foreign is applied to countries more remote than an adjacent territory; as a foreign market; a foreign prince. In the United States, all transatlantic countries are foreign.
Produced in a distant country or jurisdiction; coming from another country; as foreign goods; goods of foreign manufacture; a foreign minister.
Remote; not belonging; not connected; with to or from. You dissemble; the sentiments you express are foreign to your heart. This design is foreign from my thoughts.
Impertinent; not pertaining; not to the purpose. The observation is foreign from the subject under consideration.
foreigner: for’aner. A person born in a foreign country, or without the country or jurisdiction of which one speaks. A Spaniard is a foreigner in France and England. All men not born in the United States are to them foreigners, and they are aliens till naturalized. A naturalized person is a citizen; but we still call him a foreigner by birth.
foreignness: for’anness. Remoteness; want of relation; as the foreignness of a subject from the main business.
fore-imagine: To conceive or fancy before proof, or beforehand.
forejudge: To prejudge; to judge beforehand, or before hearing the facts and proof.
In law, to expel from a court, for malpractice or non-appearance. When an attorney is sued, and called to appear in court, if he declines, he is forejudged, and his name is struck from the rolls.
foreknow: To have previous knowledge of; to foresee. Who would the miseries of man foreknow? For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son.Romans 8:29.
foreknowledge: Knowledge of a thing before it happens; prescience. If I foreknew, foreknowledge had no influence on their fault.
forel: A kind of parchment for the cover of books.
foreland: A promontory or cape; a point of land extending into the sea some distance from the line of the shore; a head land; as the North and South Foreland in Kent, in England.
forelay: To lay wait for; to entrap by ambush.
foreleader: One who leads others by his example.
forelock: The lock or hair that grows from the forepart of the head. Take time by the forelock.
In sea language, a little flat pointed wedge of iron, used at the end of a bolt, to retain it firmly in its place.
foreman: The first or chief man; particularly, the chief man of a jury, who acts as their speaker.
The chief man in a printing office or other establishment, who conducts the whole work.
foremast: The mast of a ship or other vessel which is placed in the forepart or forecastle, and carries the foresail and foretop-sail yards. Foremast-men, on board of ships, the men who take in the top- sails, sling the yards, furl the sails. 5/
forementioned: Mentioned before; recited or written in a former part of the same writing or discourse.
foremost: First in place; most advanced; as the foremost troops of an army.
First in dignity. In honor he held the foremost rank.
forenamed: Mentioned before in the same writing or discourse.
forenoon: The former part of the day, from the morning to meridian or noon. We usually call the first part of the day, from the dawn to the time of breakfast, or the hour of business, the morning, and from this period to noon, the forenoon. But the limits are not precisely defined by custom.
forenotice: Notice or information of an event before it happens.
forensic: Belonging to courts of judicature; used in courts or legal proceedings; as a forensic term; forensic eloquence or disputes.
foreordain: To ordain or appoint before; to preordain; to predestinate; to predetermine.
foreordination: Previous ordination or appointment; predetermination; predestination.
forepart: The part first in time; as the forepart of the day or week.
The part most advanced in place; the anterior part; as the forepart of any moving body.
The beginning; as the forepart of a series.
forepast: Past before a certain time; as forepast sins.
forepossessed: Holding formerly in possession; also, preoccupied; prepossessed; preengaged.
forequoted: Cited before; quoted in a foregoing part of the work.
forereach: uponIn navigation, to gain or advance upon in progression or motion.
forerun: To advance before; to come before as an earnest of something to follow; to introduce as a harbinger. Heaviness foreruns the good event.
To precede; to have the start of.
forerunner: A messenger sent before to give notice of the approach of others; a harbinger. My elder brothers, my forerunners came.
A prognostic; a sign foreshowing something to follow. Certain pains in the head, back and limbs are the forerunners of a fever.
foresail: A sail extended on the foreyard, which is supported by the foremast.
foresee: To see beforehand; to see or know an event before it happens; to have prescience of; to foreknow. A prudent man foreseeth the evil and hideth himself. Proverbs 22:3.
foreshame: To shame; to bring reproach on.
foreship: The forepart of a ship. Acts 27:30.
foreshorten: In painting; to shorten figures for the sake of showing those behind.
foreshortening: In painting, the act of shortening figures for the sake of showing those behind. The art of conveying to the mind the impression of the entire length of an object, when represented as viewed in an oblique or receding position.
foreshow: To show beforehand; to prognosticate. Next, like aurora, Spenser rose, whose purple blush the day foreshows.
To represent beforehand, or before it comes.
foreshrouds: The shrouds of a ship attached to the foremast.
foreside: The front side; also, a specious outside.
foresight: Prescience; foreknowledge; prognostication; the act of foreseeing.
Provident care of futurity; foreknowledge accompanied with prudence in guarding against evil.
foresignify: To signify beforehand; to betoken previously; to foreshow; to typify.
foreskin: The skin that covers the glans penis; the prepuce.
foreskirt: The loose and pendulous part of a coat before.
foreslow: To delay; to hinder; to impede; to obstruct. No stream, no wood, no mountain could foreshow their hasty pace.
forespeak: To foresay; to foreshow; to foretell or predict.
forest: An extensive wood, or a large tract of land covered with trees. In America, the word is usually applied to a wood of native growth, or a tract of woodland which has never been cultivated. It differs from wood or woods chiefly in extent. We read of the Hercynian forest, in Germany, and the forest of Ardennes, in France or Gaul.

In law, in Great Britain, a certain territory of woody grounds and pastures, privileged for wild beasts and fowls of forest, chase and warren, to rest and abide in, under the protection of the king, for his pleasure. In this sense, the word has no application in America. Forest laws, laws for governing and regulating forests, and preserving game.
To cover with trees or wood.
forestaff: An instrument used at sea, for taking the altitudes of heavenly bodies; called also cross-staff.
forestage: An ancient service paid by foresters to the king; also, the right of foresters.
forestall: To anticipate; to take beforehand. Why need a man forestall his date of grief, and run to meet what he would most avoid?
To hinder by preoccupation or prevention. I will not forestall your judgment of the rest.
In law, to buy or bargain for corn, or provisions of any kind, before they arrive at the market or fair, with intent to sell them at higher prices. This is a penal offense.
forestalled: Anticipated; hindered; purchased before arrival in market.
forestaller: One who forestalls; a person who purchases provisions before they come to the fair or market, with a view to raise the price.
forestalling: Anticipating; hindering; buying provisions before they arrive in market, with intent to sell them at high prices.
Anticipation; prevention; the act of buying provisions before they are offered in market, with intent to sell them at higher prices.
forestay: Ina ship’s rigging, a large strong rope reaching from the foremast head towards the bowsprit end, to support the mast.
forester: In England, an officer appointed to watch a forest, preserve the game, and institute suits for ttespasses.
foretaste: A taste beforehand; anticipation. The pleasures of piety are a foretaste of heaven.
To taste before possession; to have previous enjoyment or experience of something; to anticipate.
foretaster: One that tastes beforehand or before another.
foretell: To predict; to tell before an event happens; to prophesy.
To utter prediction or prophecy. All the prophets from Samuel, and those that follow after, as many as have spoken, have likewise foretold of these days. Acts 3:24.
foreteller: One who predicts or prophesies; a foreshower.
forethink: To think beforehand; to anticipate in the mind. The soul of every man perpetually does forethink thy fall.
forethought: A thinking beforehand; anticipation; prescience; premeditation.
foretoken: To foreshew; to presignify; to prognosticate. Whilst strange prodigious signs foretoken blood.
foretooth: foreteeth. One of the teeth in the forepart of the mouth; an incisor.
foretop: The hair on the forepart of the head.
That part of a woman’s headdress that is forward, or the top of a periwig.
In ships, the platform erected at the head of the foremast. In this sense, the accent on the two syllables is nearly equal.
foretop-mast: The mast erected at the head of the foremast, and at the head of which stands the foretop-gallant-mast.
forewarn: To admonish beforehand. I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear. Luke 12:5.
To inform previously; to give previous notice.
forewoman: A woman who is chief; the head woman.
foreworn: Worn out; wasted or obliterated by time or use.
forfeit: for’fit. To lose or render confiscable, by some fault, offense or crime; to lose the right to some species of property or that which belongs to one; to alienate the right to possess by some neglect or crime; as, to forfeit an estate by a breach of the condition of tenure or by treason. By the ancient laws of England, a man forfeited his estate by neglecting or refusing to fulfill the conditions on which it was granted to him, or by a breach of fealty. A man now forfeits his estate by committing treason. A man forfeits his honor or reputation by a breach of promise, and by any criminal or disgraceful act. Statutes declare that by certain acts a man shall forfeit a certain sum of money. Under the feudal system, the right to the land forfeited, vested in the lord or superior. In modern times, the right to things forfeited is generally regulated by statutes; it is vested in the state, in cor

That which is forfeited or lost, or the right to which is alienated by a crime, offense, neglect of duty, or breach of contract; hence, a fine; a mulet; a penalty. He that murders pays the forfeit of his life. When a statute creates a penalty for a transgression, either in money or in corporal punishment, the offender who, on conviction, pays the money or suffers the punishment, pays the forfeit.
used for forfeited. Lost or alienated for an offense or crime; liable to penal seizure. And his long toils were forfeit for a look.
forfeitable: Liable to be forfeited; subject to forfeiture. For the future, uses shall be subject to the statutes of mortmain, and forfeitable like the lands themselves.
forfeited: Lost or alienated by an offense, crime or breach of condition.
forfeiting: Alienating or losing, as a right by an offense, crime or breach of condition.
forfeiture: The act of forfeiting; the losing of some right, privilege, estate, honor, office or effects, by an offense, crime, breach of condition or other act. In regard to property, forfeiture is a loss of the right to possess, but not generally the actual possession, which is to be transferred by some subsequent process. In the feudal system, a forfeiture of lands gave him in reversion or remainder a right to enter.
That which is forfeited; an estate forfeited; a fine or mulet. The prince enriched his treasury by fines and forfeitures.
forge: A furnace in which iron or other metal is heated and hammered into form. A larger forge is called with us iron-works. Smaller forges consisting of a bellows so placed as to cast a stream of air upon ignited coals, are of various forms and users. Armies have travelling forges, for repairing gun-carriages.
Any place where any thing is made or shaped.
The act of beating or working iron or steel; the manufacture of metalline bodies. In the greater bodies the forge was easy.
To form by heating and hammering; to beat into any particular shape, as a metal.
To make by any means. Names that the schools forged, and put into the mouths of scholars.
forged: Hammered; beaten into shape; made; counterfeited.
forgery: The act of forging or working metal into shape. In this sense, rarely or never now used.

The act of falsifying; the crime of counterfeiting; as the forgery of coin, or of bank notes, or of a bond. Forgery may consist in counterfeiting a writing, or in setting a false name to it, to the prejudice of another person.
That which is forged or counterfeited. Certain letters, purporting to be written by Gen. Washington, during the revolution, were forgeries.
forget: To lose the remembrance of; to let go from the memory. Bless the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits. Psalm 103:2.
To slight; to neglect. Can a woman forget her sucking child? Yea, they may forget, yet will I not forget thee. Isaiah 49:15.
forgetful: Apt to forget; easily losing the remembrance of. A forgetful man should use helps to strengthen his memory.
Heedless; careless; neglectful; inattentive.< Be not forgetful to entertain strangers. Hebrews 13:2.
Causing to forget; inducing oblivion; oblivious; as forgetful draughts.
forgetfulness: The quality of losing the remembrance or recollection of a thing; or rather, the quality of being apt to let any thing slip from the mind.
Loss of remembrance or recollection; a ceasing to remember; oblivion. A sweet forgetfulness of human care.
Neglect; negligence; careless omission; inattention; as forgetfulness of duty.
forgetter: One that forgets; a heedless person. 5ffs
forgetting: The act of forgetting; forgetfulness; inattention.
forgive: To pardon; to remit, as an offense or debt; to overlook an offense, and treat the offender as not guilty. The original and proper phrase is to forgive the offense, to send it away, to reject it, that is, not to impute it, the offender. But by an easy transition, we also use the phrase, to forgive the person offending. Forgive us our debts. If we forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly father will also forgive you. Matthew 6:14. As savages never forget a favor, so they never forgive an injury . It is to be noted that pardon, like forgive, may be followed by the name or person, and by the offense; but remit can be followed by the offense only. We forgive or pardon the man, but we do not remit him.
To remit as a debt, fine or penalty.
forgiveness: The act of forgiving; the pardon of an offender, by which he is considered and treated as not guilty. The forgiveness of enemies is a christian duty.
The pardon or remission of an offense or crime; as the forgiveness of sin or of injuries.
Disposition to pardon; willingness to forgive. And mild forgiveness intercede to stop the coming blow.
Remission of a debt, fine or penalty.
forgiving: Pardoning; remitting. 2.a. Disposed to forgive; inclined to overlook offenses; mild; merciful; compassionate; as a forgiving temper.
forisfamiliate: To renounce a legal title to a further share of paternal inheritance. Literally, to put one’s’ self out of the family.
forisfamiliation: When a child has received a portion of his father’s estate, and renounces all title to a further shar, his act is called forisfamiliation, and he is said to be forisfamiliated.
fork: an instrument consisting of a handle, and a blade of metal, divided into two or more points or prongs, used for lifting or pitching any thing; as a tablefork for feeding; a pitchfork; a dungforkforks are also made of ivory, wood or other material.
A point; as a thunderbolt with three forks. Shakespeare uses it for the point of an arrow.
Forks, in the plural, the point where a road parts into two; and the point where a river divides, or rather where two rivers meet and unite in one stream. Each branch is called a fork.
To shoot into blades, as corn.
to divide into two; as, a road forks.
forked: Raised, pitched or dug with a fork.
a. Opening into two or more parts, points or shoots; as a forked tongue; the forked lightning.
forkedness: The quality of opening into two or more parts.
forktail: A salmon, in his fourth year’s growth.
forky: Forked; furcated; opening into two or more parts, shoots or points; as a forky tongue.
forlorn: Deserted; destitute; stripped or deprived; forsaken. Hence, lost; helpless; wretched; solitary. Of fortune and of hope at once forlorn. To live again in these wild woods forlorn. For here forlorn and lost I tread.
Taken away. Obs. When as night hath us of light forlorn.

Small; despicable; in a ludicrous sense. Forlorn hope, properly, a desperate case; hence in military affairs, a detachment of men appointed to lead in an assault, to storm a counterscarp, enter a breach, or perform other service attended with uncommon peril.9Y
forlornness: Destitution; misery; a forsaken or wretched condition.
form: The shape or external appearance of a body; the figure, as defined by lines and angles; that manner of being peculiar to each body, which exhibits it to the eye as distinct from every other body. Thus we speak of the form of a circle, the form of a square or triangle, a circular form, the form of the head or of the human body, a handsome form, an ugly form, a frightful form. Matter is the basis or substratum of bodies, form is the particular disposition of matter in each body which distinguishes its appearance from that of every other body. The form of his visage was changed. Daniel 3:19. After that he appeared in another form to two of them, as they walked. Mark 16:12.
Manner of arranging particulars; disposition of particular things; as a form of words or expressions.
Model; draught; pattern. Hold fast the form of sound words, which thou hast heard of me. Timothy 1:13.
Beauty; elegance; splendor; dignity. He hath no form nor comeliness. Isaiah 53:2.
Regularity; method; order. This is a rough draught to be reduced to form.
formal: According to form; agreeable to established mode; regular; methodical.
Strictly ceremonious; precise; exact to affectation; as a man formal in his dress, his gait or deportment.
Done in due form, or with solemnity; express; according to regular method; not incidental, sudden or irregular. He gave his formal consent to the treaty.
Regular; methodical; as the formal stars.
Having the form or appearance without the substance or essence; external; as formal duty; formal worship.
formalist: One who observes forms, or practices external ceremonies. More generally,

One who regards appearances only, or observes the forms of worship, without possessing the life and spirit of religion; a hypocrite. A grave face and the regular practice of ceremonies have often gained to a formalist the reputation of piety.
formality: The practice or observance of forms. Formalities of extraordinary zeal and piety are never more studied and elaborate then in desperate designs.
Ceremony; mere conformity to customary modes.V\ . Nor was his attendance on divine offices a matter of formality and custom, but of conscience.
Established order; rule of proceeding; mode; method; as the formalities of judicial process; formalities of law.
Order; decorum to be observed; customary mode of behavior.
Customary mode of dress; habit; robe.
formally: According to established form, rule, order, rite or ceremony. A treaty was concluded and formally ratified by both parties.
Ceremoniously; stiffly; precisely; as, to be stiff and formally reserved.
In open appearance; in a visible and apparent state. You and your followers do stand formally divided against the authorized guides of the church, and the rest of the people.
Essentially; characteristically. That which formally makes this a christian grace, is the spring from which it flows.
formation: The act of forming or making; the act of creating or causing to exist; or more generally, the operation of composing, by bringing materials together, or of shaping and giving form; as the formation of the earth; the formation of a state or constitution.
Generation; production; as the formation of ideas.
The manner in which a thing is formed. Examine the peculiar formation of the heart.
In grammar, the act or manner of forming one word from another, as controller from control.

In geology, formation may signify a single mass of one kind of rock, more or less extensive, or a collection of mineral substances, formed by the same agent, under the same or similar circumstances; or it may convey the idea, that certain masses or collections of minerals were formed not only by the same agent, but also at the same time. In this latter sense the term is almost always employed.
formative: Giving form; having the power of giving form; plastic. The meanest plant cannot be raised without seeds, by any formative power residing in the soil.
In grammar, serving to form; derivative; not radical; as a termination merely formative.
formed: Made; shaped; molded; planned; arranged; combined; enacted; constituted.
formedon: A writ for the recovery of lands by statute of Westminister.
former: He that forms; a maker; an author.

Before in time; preceding another or something else in order of time; opposed to latter. Her former husband, who sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled. Deuteronomy 24:4.
Past, and frequently ancient, long past. For inquire, I pray thee, of the former age]ob 8:8.
Near the beginning; preceding; as the former part of a discourse or argument.
formerly: In time past, either in time immediately preceding, or at any indefinite distance; of old; heretofore. We formerly imported slaves from Africa. Nations formerly made slaves of prisoners taken in war.
formiate: A neutral salt, composed of the formic acid and a base.
formic: Pertaining to ants; as the formic acid, the acid of ants.
formication: A sensation of the body resembling that made by the creeping of ants on the skin.
formidable: Exciting fear or apprehension; impressing dread; adapted to excite fear and deter from approach, encounter or undertaking. It expresses less than terrible, terrific, tremendous, horrible, and frightful . They seemed to fear the formidable sight. I swell my preface into a volume, and make it formidable, when you see so many pages behind.
formidableness: The quality of being formidable, or adapted to excite dread.
formidably: In a manner to impress fear.
formless: Shapeless; without a determinate form; wanting regularity of shape.
formula: A prescribed form; a rule or model.
In church affairs, a confession of faith.
In mathematics, a general expression for resolving certain cases or problems.
formule: A prescribed form; a rule or model.
In church affairs, a confession of faith.
In mathematics, a general expression for resolving certain cases or problems.
formulary: A book containing stated and prescribed forms, as of oaths, declarations, prayers and the like; a book of precedents.
fornicate: Arched; vaulted like an oven or furnace.
To commit lewdness, as an unmarried man or woman, or as a married man with an unmarried woman.' . If a brahman fornicate with a Nayr woman, he shall not thereby lose his cast.
fornicated: Arched; vaulted like an oven or furnace.
fornication: The incontinence or lewdness of unmarried persons, male or female; also, the criminal conversation of a married man with an unmarried woman.' .
Idolatry; a forsaking of the true God, and worshipping of idols. Chronicles 21:11; Revelation 19:2,
fornicator: An unmarried person, male or female, who has criminal conversation with the other sex; also, a married man who has sexual commerce with an unmarried woman.
forpass: To go by; to pass unnoticed. Obs.
forpine: To pine or waste away. Obs.
forsake: To quit or leave entirely; to desert; to abandon; to depart from. Friends and flatterers forsake us in adversity. Forsake the foolish, and live. Proverbs 9:6.
To abandon; to renounce; to reject. If his children forsake my law, and walk not in my judgments - Psalm 89:30. Cease from anger, and forsake wrath. Psalm 37:8.
To leave; to withdraw from; to fail. In anger, the color forsakes the cheeks. In severe trials, let not fortitude forsake you.
In scripture, God forsakes his people, when he withdraws his aid, or the light of his countenance.
forsooth: In truth; in fact; certainly; very well. A fit man, forsooth, to govern a realm. It is generally used in an ironical or contemptuous sense.
forswear: To reject or renounce upon oath.

To deny upon oath. Like innocence, and as serenely bold as truth, how loudly he forswears thy gold. To forswear one’s self, is to swear falsely; to perjure one’s self. Thou shalt not forswear thyself. Matthew 5:33.
To swear falsely; to commit perjury.
forswearer: One who rejects on oath; one who is perjured; one that swears a false oath.
forsworn: of forswear. Renounced on oath; perjured.
fort: A fortified place; usually, a small fortified place; a place surrounded with a ditch, rampart, and parapet, or with palisades, stockades, or other means of defense; also, any building or place fortified for security against an enemy; a castle.
A strong side, opposed to weak side or foible.
forte: A direction to sign with strength of voice.
forted: Furnished with forts; guarded by forts.
forth: Forward; onward in time; in advance; as from that day forth; from that time forth.
Forward in place or order; as one, two, three, and so forth.
Out; aboard; noting progression or advance from a state of confinement; as, the plants in spring put forth leaves. When winter past, and summer scarce begun, invites them forth to labor in the sun.
Out; away; beyond the boundary of a place; as, send him forth of France.
Out into public view, or public character. Your country calls you forth into its service.
forth-coming: Ready to appear; making appearance. Let the prisoner be forth-coming.
forth-issuing: Issuing; coming out; coming forward as from a covert.
forthright: Straight forward; in a straight direction. Obs.
forthwith: Immediately there fell from his eyes as it had been scales; and he received his sight forthwith. Acts 9:18.
fortieth: The fourth tenth; noting the number next after the thirty ninth.
fortification: The art or science of fortifying places to defend them against an enemy, by means of moats, ramparts, parapets and other bulwarks.
The works erected to defend a place against attack.
A fortified place; a fort; a castle.
fortifier: One who erects works for defense.
One who strengthens, supports and upholds; that which strengthens.
fortify: To surround with a wall, ditch, palisades or other works, with a view to defend against the attacks of an enemy; to strengthen and secure by forts, batteries and other works of art; as, to fortify a city, town or harbor.
To strengthen against any attack; as, to fortify the mind against sudden calamity.
To confirm; to add strength and firmness to; as, to fortify an opinion or resolution; to fortify hope or desire.
To furnish with strength or means of resisting force, violence or assault.
fortin: A little fort; a field fort; a sconce.
fortitude: That strength or firmness of mind or soul which enables a person to encounter danger with coolness and courage, or to bear pain or adversity without murmuring, depression or despondency. Fortitude is the basis or source of genuine courage or intrepidity in danger, of patience in suffering, of forbearance under injuries, and of magnanimity in all conditions of life. We sometimes confound the effect with the cause, and use fortitude as synonymous with courage or patience; but courage is an active virtue or vice, and patience is the effect of fortitude. Fortitude is the guard and support of the other virtues.
fortnight: fort’nit. The space of fourteen days; two weeks.
fortress: Any fortified place; a fort; a castle; a strong hold; a place of defense or security. The English have a strong fortress on the rock of Gibraltar, or that rock is a fortress.
Defense; safety; security; . The Lord is my rock, and my fortress.Psalm 18:2.
To furnish with fortresses; to guard; to fortify.
fortressed: Defended by a fortress; protected; secured.
fortuitous: Accidental; casual; happening by chance; coming or occurring unexpectedly, or without any known cause. We speak of fortuitous events, when they occur without our foreseeing or expecting them, and of a fortuitous concourse of atoms, when we suppose the concourse not to result from the design and power of a controlling agent. But an event cannot be in fact fortuitous.
fortuitousness: The quality of being accidental; accident; chance.
fortunate: Coming by good luck or favorable chance; bringing some unexpected good; as a fortunate event; a fortunate concurrence of circumstances; a fortunate ticket in a lottery.

Lucky; successful; receiving some unforeseen or unexpected good, or some good which was not dependent on one’s own skill or efforts; as a fortunate adventurer in a lottery. I was most fortunate thus unexpectedly to meet my friend.

Successful; happy; prosperous; receiving or enjoying some good in consequence of efforts, but where the event was uncertain, and not absolutely in one’s power. The brave man is usually fortunate. We say, a fortunate competitor for a fair lady, or for a crown.
fortunately: Luckily; successfully; happily; by good fortune, or favorable chance or issue.
fortune: Properly, chance; accident; luck; the arrival of something in a sudden or unexpected manner. Hence the heathens deified chance, and consecrated temples and altars to the goddess. Hence the modern use of the word, for a power supposed to distribute the lots of life, according to her own humor. Though fortune’s malice overthrow my state.
The good or ill that befalls man. In you the fortune of Great Britain lies.
Success, good or bad; event. Our equal crimes shall equal fortune give.
The chance of life; means of living; wealth. His father dying, he was driven to London to seek his fortune.
Estate; possessions, as a gentleman of small fortune.
fortunebook: A book to be consulted to discover future events.
fortune-hunter: A man who seeks to marry a woman with a large portion, with a view to enrich himself.
fortuneless: Luckless; also, destitute of a fortune or portion.
fortunetell: To tell or pretend to tell the future events of one’s life; to reveal futurity.
fortuneteller: One who tells or pretends to foretell the events of one’s life; an impostor who deceives people by pretending to a knowledge of future events.
fortunetelling: Telling the future events of one’s life.
The act or practice of foretelling the future fortune or events of one’s life, which is a punishable crime.
forty: An indefinite number; a colloquial use. A, B and C, and forty more.
forum: In Rome, a public place, where causes were judicially tried, and orations delivered to the people; also, a market place. Hence,
A tribunal; a court; any assembly empowered to hear and decide causes; also.
forwander: To wander away; to rove wildly.
forward: Toward a part or place before or in front; onward; progressively; opposed to backward. Go forward; move forward. He ran backward and forward. In a ship, forward denotes toward the forepart.
Near or at the forepart; in advance of something else; as the forward gun in a ship, or the forward ship in a fleet; the forward horse in a team.
Ready; prompt; strongly inclined. Only they would that we should remember the poor; the same which I also was forward to do. Galatians 2:10.
Ardent; eager; earnest; violent. Or lead the forward youth to noble war.
Bold; confident; less reserved or modest than is proper; in an ill sense; as, the boy is too forward for his years.
forwarded: Advanced; promoted; aided in progress; quickened; sent onward; transmitted.
forwarder: He that promotes, or advanced in progress.
forwarding: Advancing; promoting; aiding in progress; accelerating in growth; sending onwards; transmitting.
forwardness: Cheerful readiness; promptness. It expresses more than willingness. We admire the forwardness of christians in propagating the gospel.
Eagerness; ardor. It is sometimes difficult to restrain the forwardness of youth.
Boldness; confidence; assurance; want of due reserve or modesty. In France it is usual to bring children into company, and cherish in them, from their infancy, a kind of forwardness and assurance.
A state of advance beyond the usual degree; as the forwardness of spring or of corn.
foss: A ditch or moat; a word used in fortification.
In anatomy, a kind of cavity in a bone, with a large aperture.
fossil: Dug out of the earth; as fossil coal; fossil salt. The term fossil is now usually appropriated to those inorganic substances, which have become penetrated by earthy or metallic particles. Thus we say, fossil shells, fossil bones, fossil wood.
That may be taken from the earth by digging.

A substance dug from the earth, or penetrated with earthy or metallic particles. Fossils are native or extraneous. Native fossils are minerals, properly so called, as earth, salts, combustibles and metallic bodies. Extraneous fossils are bodies of vegetable or animal origin accidentally buried in the early, as plants, shells, bones and other substances, many of which are petrified.
fossil-copal: Highgate resin; a resinous substance found in perforating the bed of blue clay at Highgate, near London. It appears to be a true vegetable gum or resin, partly changed by remaining in the earth.
fossilist: One who studies the nature and properties of fossils; one who is versed in the science of fossils.
fossilization: The act or process of converting into a fossil or petrification.
fossilize: To convert into a fossil; as, to fossilize bones or wood.
To become or be changed into a fossil.
fossilogy: A discourse or treatise on fossils; also, the science of fossils.
fossroad: A Roman military way in England, leading from Totness through Exeter to Barton on the Humber; so called from the ditches on each side.
fossway: A Roman military way in England, leading from Totness through Exeter to Barton on the Humber; so called from the ditches on each side.
foster: To feed; to nourish; to support; to being up. Some say that ravens foster forlorn children.
To cherish; to forward; to promote growth. The genial warmth of spring fosters the plants.
To cherish; to encourage; to sustain and promote; as, to foster passion or genius.
To be nourished or trained up together.
foster-brother: A male nursed as the same breast, or fed by the same nurse.
foster-child: A child nursed by a woman not the mother, or bred bya man not the father.
foster-dam: A nurse; one that performs the office of a mother by giving food to a child.
foster-earth: Earth by which a plant is nourished, though not its native soil.
fosterer: A nurse; one that feeds and nourishes in the place of parents.
foster-father: One who takes the place of a father in feeding and educating a child.
fostering: The act of nursing, nourishing and cherishing.
foster-sister: A female nursed by the same person.
foster-son: One fed and educated, like a son, though not a son by birth.
fostress: A female who feeds and cherishes; a nurse.
fother: A weight of lead containing eight pigs, and every pig twenty one stone and a half. But the fother is of different weights. With the plumbers in London it is nineteen hundred and a half, and at the mines, it is twenty two hundred and a half.

To endeavor to stop a leak in the bottom of a ship, while afloat, by letting down a sail by the corners, and putting chopped yarn, oakum, wool, cottonBetween it and the ship’s sides. These substances are sometimes sucked into the cracks and the leak stopped.
fothering: The operation of stopping leaks in a ship, as above.
fougade: In the art of war, a little mine, in the form of a well, 8 or 10 feet wide, and 10 or 12 deep, dug under some work, fortification or post, charged with sacks of powder and covered with stones or earth, for destroying the works by explosion.
fought: pret, and pp. of fight; pron. faut.
foul: To make filthy; to defile; to daub; to dirty; to bemire; to soil; as, to foul the clothes; to foul the face or hands. Ezekiel 34:18.
Covered with or containing extraneous matter which is injurious, noxious or offensive; filthy; dirty; not clean; as a foul cloth; foul hands; a foul chimney. My face is foul with weeping. Job 76:76.
Turbid; thick; muddy; as foul water; a foul stream.
Impure; polluted; as a foul mouth.
Impure; scurrilous; obscene or profane; as foul words; foul language.
foulfaced: Having an ugly or hateful visage.
foully: Filthily; nastily; hatefully; © scandalously; disgracefully; shamefully. I foully wronged him; do, forgive me.
Unfairly; not honestly. Thou play’dst most foully for it.
foulmouthed: Using language scurrilous, opprobrious, obscene or profane; uttering abuse, or profane or obscene words; accustomed to use bad language. So foulmouthed a witness never appeared in any cause.
foulness: The quality of being foul or filthy; filthiness; defilement.

The quality or state of containing or being covered with any thing extraneous which is noxious or offensive; as the foulness of a cellar, or of a well; the foulness of a musket; the foulness of a ship’s bottom.
Pollution; impurity. There is not so chaste a nation as this, nor so free from all pollution or foulness.
Hatefulness; atrociousness; as the foulness of a deed.
Ugliness; deformity. The foulness of the’ infernal form to hide.
foulspoken: Using profane, scurrilous or obscene language.
found: and pp. of find. I am found of them that sought me not. Isaiah 65:7.
To lay the basis of any thing; to set, or place, as on something solid for support. It fell not, for it was founded on a rock. Matthew 7:25.
To begin and build; to lay the foundation, and raise a superstructure; as, to found a city.
To set or place; to establish, as on something solid or durable; as, to found a government on principles of liberty.
To begin; to form or lay the basis; as, to found a college or a library. Sometimes to endow is equivalent to found.
foundation: The basis of an edifice; that part of a building which lies on the ground; usually a wall of stone which supports the edifice.
The act of fixing the basis.

The basis or ground work, or any thing; that on which any thing stands, and by which it is supported. A free government has its foundation in the choice and consent of the people to be governed. Christ is the foundation of the church. Behold, I lay in Zion for a foundation, a stone - a precious cornerstone. Isaiah 28:16. Other foundation can no man lay than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ. 7 Corinthians 3:11.
Original; rise; as the foundation of the world.
Endowment; a donation or legacy appropriated to support an institution, and constituting a permanent fund, usually for a charitable purpose.
founded: Set; fixed; established on a basis; begun and built.
founder: One that founds, establishes and erects; one that lays a foundation; as the founder of a temple or city.
One who begins; an author; one from whom any thing originates; as the founder of a sect of philosophers; the founder of a family or race.
One who endows; one who furnishes a permanent fund for the support of an institution; as the founder of a college or hospital.
A caster; one who casts metals in various forms; as a founder of cannon, belles, hardware, printing types.
In seamen’s language, to fill or be filled and sink, as a ship.
foundered: Made lame in the feet by inflammation and extreme tenderness.
foundery: The art of casting metals into various forms for use; the casting of statues.
The house and works occupied in casting metals; as a foundery of bells, of hollow ware, of cannon, of types.
foundling: A deserted or exposed infant; a child found without a parent or owner. A hospital for such children is called a foundling hospital.
foundress: A female founder; a woman who founds or establishes, or who endows with a fund.
fount: A spring, or source of water; properly, a spring or issuing of water from the earth. This word accords in sense with well, in our mother tongue; but we now distinguish the, applying fountain to a natural spring of water, and well to an artificial pit of water, issuing from the interior of the earth.
A jet; a spouting of water; an artificial spring.
The head or source of a river.
Original; first principle or cause; the source of any thing. Almighty God, the fountain of all goodness. Fount of types.
fountain: A spring, or source of water; properly, a spring or issuing of water from the earth. This word accords in sense with well, in our mother tongue; but we now distinguish the, applying fountain to a natural spring of water, and well to an artificial pit of water, issuing from the interior of the earth.
A jet; a spouting of water; an artificial spring.
The head or source of a river.
Original; first principle or cause; the source of any thing. Almighty God, the fountain of all goodness. Fount of types.
fountainless: Having no fountain; wanting a spring. A barren desert fountainless and dry.
fountain-tree: In the Canary isles, a tree which distills water from its leaves, in sufficient abundance for the inhabitants near it.
fountful: Full of springs; as fountful Ida.
four: Twice two; denoting the sum or two and two.
fourfold: Four double; quadruple; four times told; as a fourfold division. He shall restore the lamb fourfold.2 Samuel 12:6.
fourfooted: Quadruped; having four feet; as the horse and the ox.
fourscore: Four times twenty; eighty. It is used elliptically for fourscore years; as a man of fourscore.
foursquare: Having four sides and four angles equal; quadrangular.
fourteen: Four and ten; twice seven. bet
a2. Four and ten; twice seven.
fourteenth: The ordinal of fourteen; the fourth after the tenth.
fourth: The ordinal of four; the next after the third.
In music, an interval composed of two tones and a semitone. Three full tones compose a triton, or fourth redundant.
fourwheeled: Having or running on four wheels.
fovilla: A fine substance, imperceptible to the naked eye, emitted from the pollen of flowers.
fowl: To catch or kill wild fowls for game or food; as by means of bird-lime, decoys, nets and snares, or by pursuing them with hawks, or by shooting.

A flying or winged animal; the generic name of certain animals that move through the air by the aid of wings. Fowls have two feet, are covered with feathers, and have wings for flight. Bird is a young fowl or chicken, and may well be applied to the smaller species of fowls. But it has usurped the place of fowl, and is used improperly as the generic term. Fowl is used as a collective noun. We dined on fish and fowl. Let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air. Genesis 1:26. But this use in America is not frequent. We generally use the pluralThe word is colloquially used for poultry, or rather, in a more limited sense, for barn door fowls.
fowler: A sportsman who pursues wild fowls, or takes or kills them for food.
fowling: The art or practice of catching or shooting fowls; also
fowlingpiece: A light gun for shooting fowls.
fox: An animal of the genus Canis, with a straight tail, yellowish or straw-colored hair, and erect ears. This animal burrows in the earth, is remarkable for his cunning, and preys on lambs, geese, hens or other small animals.
In seaman’s language, a seizing made by twisting several rope- yarns together.
Formerly, a cant expression for a sword.
foxchase: The pursuit of a fox with hounds.
foxery: Behavior like that of a fox.
foxevil: A kind of disease in which the fair falls off.
foxglove: The name of a plant, the Digitalis.
foxhunt: The chase or hunting of a fox.
foxhunter: One who hunts or pursues foxes with hounds.
foxish: Resembling a fox in qualities; cunning.
foxlike: Resembling a fox in qualities; cunning.
foxship: The character or qualities of a fox; cunning.
foxtail: A species of grass, the Alopecurus.
foxtrap: A trap, or a gin or snare to catch foxes.
fracas: An uproar; a noisy quarrel; a disturbance.
fraction: The act of breaking or state of being broken, especially by violence.

In arithmetic and algebra, a broken part of an integral or integer; any division of a whole number or unit, as 2/5, two fifths, 1/4, one fourth, which are called vulgar fractions. In these, the figure above the line is called the numerator, and the figure below the line the denominator. In decimal fractions, the denominator is a unit, or 1, with as many cyphers annexed, as the numerator has places. They are commonly expressed by writing the numerator only, with a point before it by which it is separated from the whole number; thus .5, which denotes five tenths, 5/10, or half the whole number; .25, that is 25/100, or a fourth part of the whole number.
fractional: Belonging to a broken number; comprising a part or the parts of a unit; as fractional numbers.
fractious: Apt to break out into a passion; apt to quarrel; cross; snappish; as a fractious man.
fracture: A breach in any body, especially a breach caused by violence; a rupture of a solid body.
In surgery, the rupture or disruption of a bone. A fracture is simple or compound; simple, when the bone only is divided; compound, when the bone is broken, with a laceration of the integuments.
In mineralogy, the manner in which a mineral breaks, and by which its texture is displayed; as a compact fracture; a fibrous fracture; foliated, striated or conchoidal fracture.
To break; to burst asunder; to crack; to separate continuous parts; as, to fracture a bone; to fracture the skull.
fragile: Brittle; easily broken. The stalk of ivy is tough, and not fragile.
Weak; liable to fail; easily destroyed; as fragile arms.
fragment: A part broken off; a piece separated from any thing by breaking. Gather up the fragments that remain, that nothing is lost. John 6:12.
A part separated from the rest; an imperfect part; as fragments of ancient writings.
A small detached portion; as fragments of time.
fragor: Aloud and sudden sound; the report of any thing bursting; a loud harsh sound; a crash.
A strong or sweet scent. Obs.
fragrance: Sweetness of smell; that quality of bodies which affects the olfactory nerves with an agreeable sensation; pleasing scent; grateful odor. Eve separate he spies, vailed in a cloud of fragrance - . The goblet crown’d, breathed aromatic fragrances around.
fragrancy: Sweetness of smell; that quality of bodies which affects the olfactory nerves with an agreeable sensation; pleasing scent; grateful odor. Eve separate he spies, vailed in a cloud of fragrance - . The goblet crown’d, breathed aromatic fragrances around.
fragrant: Sweet of smell; odorous. Fragrant the fertile earth after soft showers.
frail: Weak; infirm; liable to fail and decay; subject to casualties; easily destroyed; perishable; not firm or durable. That I may know how frail I am. Psalm 39:4.
Weak in mind or resolution; liable to error deception. Man is frail, and prone to evil.
Weak; easily broken or overset; as a frail bark.
frailness: Weakness; infirmity; as the frailness of the body.
frailty: Weakness of resolution; infirmity; liableness to be deceived or seduced. God knows our frailty, and pities our weakness.
Fault proceeding from weakness; foible; sin of infirmity; in this sense it has a plural.
fraise: In fortification, a defense consisting of pointed stakes driven into the retrenchments, parallel to the horizon.
A pancake with bacon in it. Obs.
frame: To fit or prepare and unite several parts in a regular structure or entire thing; to fabricate by orderly construction and union of various parts; as, to frame a house or other building.
To fit one thing to another; to adjust; to make suitable.
To make; to compose; as, to frame a law. For thou art framed of the firm truth of valor.
To regulate; to adjust; to shape; to conform; as, to frame our lives according to the rules of the gospel. WWV”AD .
To form and digest by thought; as, to frame ideas in the mind. How many excellent reasonings are framed in the mind of a man of wisdom and study in a length of years!
framed: Fitted and united in due form; made; composed; devised; adjusted.
framer: One who frames; a maker; a contriver.
framing: Fitting and joining in due construction; making; fabricating; composing; adjusting; inventing; contriving.
franchise: A particular privilege or right granted by a prince or sovereign to an individual or to a number of persons; as the right to be a body corporate with perpetual succession; the right to hold a court leet or other court; to have waifs, wrecks, treasure-treve, or forfeitures. So the right to vote for governor, senators and representatives, is a franchise belonging to citizens, and not enjoyed by aliens. The right to establish a bank, is a franchise.
Exemption from a burden or duty to which others are subject.
The district or jurisdiction to which a particular privilege extends; the limits of an immunity.
An asylum or sanctuary, where persons are secure from arrest. Churches and monasteries in Spain are franchises for criminals.
franchisement: Release from burden or restriction; freedom.
francic: Pertaining to the Franks or French.
franciscan: Belonging to the order of St. Francis.
One of the order of St. Francis; an order of monks founded by him in 1209. They are called also Gray Friars.
frangibility: The state or quality of being frangible.
frangible: That may be broken; brittle; fragile; easily broken.
franion: A paramour, or a boon companion.
frank: Open; ingenuous; candid; free in uttering real sentiments; not reserved; using no disguise. Young persons are usually frank; old persons are more reserved.
Open; ingenuous; as a frank disposition or heart.
Free; without conditions or compensation; as a frank gift.
franc: An ancient coin of France. The value of the gold frank was something more than that of the gold crown. The silver franc was in value a third of the gold one. The gold coin is no longer in circulation. The present franc or frank, is a silver coin of the value nearly of nineteen cents, or ten pence sterling.
A letter which is exempted from postage; or the writing which renders it free.
frankalmoigne: frankalmoin’. Free alms; in English law, a tenure by which a religious corporation holds lands to them and their successors forever, on condition of praying for the souls of the donor.
frankchase: A liberty of free chase, whereby persons having lands within the compass of the same, are prohibited to cut down any woodout of the view of the forester. Free chase, is the liberty of keeping beasts of chase or royal game therein, protected even from the owner of the land himself, with a power of hunting them thereon.
frankfee: Freehold; a holding of lands in fee simple.
frankincense: A dry resinous substance in pieces or drops, of a pale yellowish white color, of a bitterish acrid taste, and very inflammable; used as a perfume.
franklaw: Free or common law, or the benefit a person has by it.
franklinite: A mineral compound of iron, zinc and manganese, found in New Jersey, and named from Dr. Franklin.
frankly: Openly; freely; ingenuously; without reserve, constraint or disguise; as, to confess one’s faults frankly.
frankmarriage: A tenure in tail special; or an estate of inheritance given to a person, together with a wife, and descendible to the heirs of their two bodies begotten.
frankness: Plainness of speech; candor; freedom in communication; openness; ingenuousness. He told me his opinions’ with frankness.
Fairness; freedom from art or craft; as frankness of dealing.
frankpledge: A pledge or surety for the good behavior of freemen. Anciently in England, a number of neighbors who were bound for each other's good behavior.
franktenement: An estate of freehold; the possession of the soil by a freeman.
frantic: Mad; raving; furious; outrageous; wild and disorderly; distracted; as a frantic person; frantic with fear or grief.
Characterized by violence, fury and disorder; noisy; mad; wild; irregular; as the frantic rites of Bacchus.
frap: In seamen’s language, to cross and draw together the several parts of a tackle to increase the tension.
fraternal: Brotherly; pertaining to brethren; becoming brothers; as fraternal love or affection; a fraternal embrace.
fraternity: The state or quality of a brother; brotherhood.
A body of men associated for their common interest or pleasure; a company; a brotherhood; a society; as the fraternity of free masons.
Men of the same class, profession, occupation or character. With what terms of respect knaves and sots will speak of their own fraternity.
fraternization: The act of associating and holding fellowship as brethren.
fraternize: To associate or hold fellowship as brothers, or as men of like occupation or character.
fratricide: The crime of murdering a brother.
One who murders or kills a brother.
fraud: Deceit; deception; trick; artifice by which the right or interest of another is injured; a stratagem intended to obtain some undue advantage; an attempt to gain or the obtaining of an advantage over another by imposition or immoral means, particularly deception in contracts, or bargain and sale, either by stating falsehoods, or suppressing truth. If success a lover’s toil attends, who asks if force or fraud obtained his ends.
fraudful: Deceitful in making bargains; trickish; treacherous; applied to persons.
Containing fraud or deceit; applied to things.
fraudfully: Deceitfully; with intention to deceive and gain an undue advantage; trickishly; treacherously; by stratagem.
fraudulence: Deceitfulness; trickishness in making bargains, or in social concerns.
fraudulency: Deceitfulness; trickishness in making bargains, or in social concerns.
fraudulent: Deceitful in making contracts; trickish; applied to persons.
Containing fraud; founded on fraud; proceeding from fraud; as a fraudulent bargain.
Deceitful; treacherous; obtained or performed by artifice.
fraudulently: By fraud; by deceit; by artifice or imposition.
fraught: Laden; loaded; charged; as a vessel richly fraught with goods from India. This sense is used in poetry; but in common business, freighted only is used.
Filled; stored; full; as a scheme fraught with mischief; the scriptures are fraught with excellent precepts.
To load; to fill; to crowd. Obs.
fray: A boil, quarrel or violent riot, that puts men in fear. This is the vulgar word for affray, and the sense seems to refer the word to Fr. effrayer.
A combat; a battle; also, a single combat or duel.
A rub; a fret or chafe in cloth; a place injured by rubbing.
freak: Literally, a sudden starting or change of place. Hence,
A sudden causeless change or turn of the mind; a whim or fancy; a capricious prank. She is restless and peevish, and sometimes in a freak will instantly change her habitation.
To variegate; to checker. Freaked with many a mingled hue.
freakish: Apt to change the mind suddenly; whimsical; capricious. It may be a question, whether the wife or the woman was the more freakish of the two.
freakishly: Capriciously; with sudden change of mind, without cause.
freckle: A spot of a yellowish color in the skin, particularly on the face, neck and hands. Freckles may be natural or produced by the action of the sun on the skin, or from the jaundice.
freckled: Spotted; having small yellowish spots on the skin or surface; as a freckled face or neck.
frecklefaced: Having a face full of freckles.
freckly: Full of freckles; sprinkled with spots.
fred: Victorious peace. Our ancestors called a sanctuary, fredstole, a seat of peace.
free: Being at liberty; not being under necessity or restraint, physical or moral; a word of general application to the body, the will or mind, and to corporations.

In government, not enslaved; not in a state of vassalage or dependence; subject only to fixed laws, made by consent, and to a regular administration of such laws; not subject to the arbitrary will of a sovereign or lord; as a free state, nation or people.

Instituted by a free people, or by consent or choice of those who are to be subjects, and securing private rights and privileges by fixed laws and principles; not arbitrary or despotic; as a free constitution or government. There can be no free government without a democratical branch in the constitution.
Not imprisoned, confined or under arrest; as, the prisoner is set free.
Unconstrained; unrestrained; not under compulsion or control. A man is free to pursue his own choice; he enjoys free will.
freebench: A widow's dower in a copyhold.
freebooter: One who wanders about for plunder; a robber; a pillager; a plunderer.
freeborn: Born free; not in vassalage; inheriting liberty.
freechapel: In England, a chapel founded by the king and not subject to the jurisdiction of the ordinary. The kind may also grant license to a subject to found such a chapel. Free city, in Germany, an imperial city, not subject to a prince, but governed by its own magistrates.
freed: Set at liberty; loosed; delivered from restraint; cleared of hinderance or obstruction.
freedman: A man who has been a slave and is manumitted.
freedom: A state of exemption from the power or control of another; liberty; exemption from slavery, servitude or confinement. Freedom is personal, civil, political, and religious.
Particular privileges; franchise; immunity; as the freedom of a city .
Exemption from fate, necessity, or any constraint in consequence of predetermination or otherwise; as the freedom of the will.
Any exemption from constraint or control.
freefishery: A royal franchise or exclusive privilege of fishing in a public river.
freehold: That land or tenement which is held in fee-simple, fee-tail, or for term of life. It is of two kinds; in deed, and in law. The first is the real possession of such land or tenement; the last is the right a man has to such land or tenement, before his entry or seizure.

That land or tenement which is held in fee- simple, fee-tail, or for term of life. It is of two kinds; in deed, and in law. The first is the real possession of such land or tenement; the last is the right a man has to such land or tenement, before his entry or seizure. Freehold is also extended to such offices as a man holds in fee or for life. It is also taken in opposition to villenage. In the United States, a freehold is an estate which a man holds in his own right, subject to no superior nor to conditions.
freeholder: One who owns an estate in fee-simple, fee-tail or for life; the possessor of a freehold. Every juryman must be a freeholder.
freeing: Delivering from restraint; releasing from confinement; removing incumbrances or hinderances from any thing; clearing.
freely: At liberty; without vassalage, slavery or dependence.
Without restraint, constraint or compulsion; voluntarily. To render a moral agent accountable, he must act freely.
Plentifully; in abundance; as, to eat or drink freely.
Without scruple or reserve; as, to censure freely.
Without impediment or hinderance. Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat. Genesis 2:16.
freeman: One who enjoys liberty, or who is not subject to the will of another; one not a slave or vassal.
One who enjoys or is entitled to a franchise or peculiar privilege; as the freemen of a city or state.
freemason: One of the fraternity of masons.
freeness: The state or quality of being free, unconstrained, unconfined, unincumbered, or unobstructed.
Openness; unreservedness; frankness; ingenuousness; candor; as the freeness of a confession.
Liberality; generosity; as freeness in giving.
Gratuitousness; as the freeness of divine grace.
freeschool: A school supported by funds, in which pupils are taught without paying for tuition.
A school open to admit pupils without restriction.
freestone: Any species of stone composed of sand or grit, so called because it is easily cut or wrought.
freethinker: A softer name for a deist; an unbeliever; one who discards revelation.
