freewarren: A royal franchise or exclusive right of killing beasts and fowls of warren within certain limits.
freewill: The power of directing our own actions without restraint by necessity or fate.
freeze: pret. froze; pp. frozen, or froze.

To be congealed by cold; to be changed from a liquid to a solid state by the abstraction of heat; to be hardened into ice or a like solid body. Water freezes at the temperature of 32 degrees above zero by Fahrenheit’s thermometer. Mercury freezes at 40 degrees below zero.
To be of that degree of cold at which water congeals.
To chill; to stagnate, or to retire from the extreme vessels; as, the blood freezes in the veins.
To be chilled; to shiver with cold.
freight: The cargo, or any part of the cargo of a ship; lading; that which is carried by water. The freight of a ship consists of cotton; the ship has not a full freight; the owners have advertised for freight; freight will be paid for by the ton.
Transportation of goods. We paid four dollars a ton for the freight from London to Barcelona.
The hire of a ship, or money charged or paid for the transportation of goods. After paying freight and charges, the profit is trifling.
To load with goods, as a ship or vessel of any kind, for transporting them from one place to another. We freighted the ship for Amsterdam; the ship was freighted with flour for Havana.
freighted: Loaded, as a ship or vessel.
freighter: One who loads a ship, or one who charters and loads a ship.
freighting: Loading, as a ship or vessel.
freisleben: A mineral of a blue or bluish gray color, brittle and soft to the touch.
french: Pertaining to France or its inhabitants. French Chalk, scaly talc, a variety of indurated talc, in masses composed of small scales; its color is pearly white or grayish.
The language spoken by the people of France.
french-horn: A wind instrument of music made of metal.
frenchify: To make French; to infect with the manner of the French.
frenzy: Madness; distraction; rage; or any violent agitation of the mind approaching to distraction. All else is towering frenzy and distraction.
frequence: A crowd; a throng; a concourse; an assembly.
frequency: A return or occurrence of a thing often repeated at short intervals. The frequency of crimes abates our horror at the commission; the frequency of capital punishments tends to destroy their proper effect.
frequent: Often seen or done; often happening at short intervals; often repeated or occurring. We made frequent visits to the hospital .
Used often to practice any thing. He was frequent and loud in his declamations against the revolution.

To visit often; to resort to often or habitually. The man who frequents a dram-shop, an ale house, or a gaming table, is in the road to poverty, disgrace and ruin. He frequented the court of Augustus.
frequentative: In grammar, signifying the frequent repetition of an action; as a frequentative verb.
frequenter: One who often visits or resorts to customarily.
frequently: Often; many times; at short intervals; commonly.
frequentness: The quality of being frequent or often repeated.
fresco: Coolness; shade; a cool refreshing state of the air; duskiness.
A picture not drawn in glaring light, but in dusk.

A method of painting in relief on walls, performed with water- colors on fresh plaster, or on a wall laid with mortar not yet dry. the colors, incorporating with the mortar, and drying with it, become very durable. It is called fesco, either because it is done on fresh plaster, or because it is used on walls and buildings in the open air.
fresh: Moving with celerity; brisk; strong; somewhat vehement; as a fresh breeze; fresh wind; the primary sense.
Having the color and appearance of young thrifty plants; lively; not impaired or faded; as when we say, the fields look fresh and green.
Having the appearance of a healthy youth; florid; ruddy; as a fresh-colored young man.
New; recently grown; as fresh vegetables.
New; recently made or obtained. We have a fresh supply of goods from the manufactory, or from India; fresh tea; fresh raisins.
freshen: To make fresh; to dulcify; to separate, as water from saline particles; to take saltiness from any thing; as, to freshen water, fish or flesh.
In seaman’s language, to apply new service to a cable; as, to freshen hawse.
To grow fresh; to lose salt or saltiness.
freshes: The mingling of fresh water with salt water in rivers or bays, or the increased current of an ebb tide by means of a flood of fresh water, flowing towards or into the sea, and discoloring the water.
A flood; an overflowing; an inundation; a freshet.
freshet: A flood or overflowing of a river, by means of heavy rains or melted snow; an inundation.
freshly: Newly; in the former state renewed; in a new or fresh state.
freshman: A novice; one in the rudiments of knowledge.
In colleges, one of the youngest class of students.
freshness: Newness; vigor; spirit; the contrary to vapidness; as the freshness of liquors or odors.
Vigor; liveliness; the contrary to a faded state; as the freshness of plants or of green fields.
Newness of strength; renewed vigor; opposed to weariness or fatigue. The Scots had the advantage both for number and freshness of men.
Coolness; invigorating quality or state. And breathe the freshness of the open air.
Color of youth and health; ruddiness. Her cheeks their freshness lose and wonted grace.
freshwater: Accustomed to sail on freshwater only, or in the coasting trade; as a freshwater sailor.
freshwatered: Newly watered; supplied with fresh water.
fret: To rub; to wear away a substance by friction; as, to fret cloth; to fret a piece of gold or other metal. \WWV”AD .
To corrode; to gnaw; to ear away; as, a worm frets the planks of a ship.
To impair; to wear away. By starts, his fretted fortunes give him hope and fear.
fretful: Disposed to fret; ill-humored; peevish; angry; in a state of vexation; as a fretful temper.
fretfulness: Peevishness; ill-humor; disposition to fret and complain.
frett: With miners, the worn side of the bank of a river.
fretted: Eaten; corroded; rubbed or worn away; agitated; vexed; made rough on the surface; variegated; ornamented with fretwork; furnished with frets.
fretting: Corroding; wearing away; agitating; vexing; making rough on the surface; variegating.
fretwork: Raised work; work adorned with frets.
friability: The quality of being easily broken, crumbled and reduced to powder.
friableness: The quality of being easily broken, crumbled and reduced to powder.
friable: Easily crumbled or pulverized; easily reduced to powder. Pumice and calcined stones are very friable.
friar: An appellation common to the monks of all orders; those who enter religious orders considering themselves as a fraternity or brotherhood. Friars are generally distinguished into four principal branches, viz: 1. Minors, gray friars or Franciscans; 2. Augustines;
Dominicans or black friars; 4. White Friars or Carmelites .
In a restricted sense, a monk who is not a priest; those friars who are in orders being called fathers.
friarlike: Like a friar; monastic; unskilled in the world.
friarly: Like a friar; untaught in the affairs of life.
friary: A monastery; a convent of friars.
Like a friar; pertaining to friars.
fricace: Meat sliced and dressed with strong sauce; also, an unguent prepared by frying things together. Obs.
fricassee: A dish of food made by cutting chickens, rabbits or other small animals into pieces, and dressing them in a frying pan, or a like utensil.
friction: The act of rubbing the surface of one body against that of another; attrition. Many bodies by friction emit light, and friction generates or evolves heat.
In mechanics, the effect of rubbing, or the resistance which a moving body meets with from the surface on which it moves.
In medicine, the rubbing of the body with the hand, or with a brush, flannel; or the rubbing of a diseased part with oil, unguent or other medicament.
friday: The sixth day of the week, formerly consecrated to Frigga.W .
friend: One who is attached to another by affection; one who entertains for another sentiments of esteem, respect and affection, which lead him to desire his company, and to seek to promote his happiness and prosperity; opposed to foe or enemy. A friend loveth at all times. Proverbs 17:17.
One not hostile; opposed to an enemy in war.
One reconciled after enmity. Let us be friends again.
A favorer; one who is propitious; as a friend to commerce; a friend to poetry; a friend to charitable institution.
friended: a. Inclined to love; well disposed.
friendless: frend’less. Destitute of friends; wanting countenance or support; forlorn.
friendlike: frend’like. Having the dispositions of a friend.
friendliness: A disposition to friendship; friendly disposition.
friendly: Having the temper and disposition of a friend; kind; favorable; disposed to promote the good of another. Thou to mankind be good and friendly still, and oft return.
Amicable. We are on friendly terms.
Not hostile; as a friendly power or state.
Favorable; propitious; salutary; promoting the good of; as a friendly breeze or gale. Excessive rains are not friendly to the ripening fruits. Temperance is friendly to longevity.
friendship: An attachment to a person, proceeding from intimate acquaintance, and a reciprocation of kind offices, or from a favorable opinion of the amiable and respectable qualities of his mind. Friendship differs from benevolence, which is good will to mankind in general, and from that love which springs from animal appetite. True friendship is a noble and virtuous attachment, springing from a pure source, a respect for worth or amiable qualities. False friendship may subsist between bad men, as between thieves and pirates. This is a temporary attachment springing from interest, and may change in a moment to enmity and rancor. There can be no friendship without confidence, and no confidence without integrity. There is little friendship in the world. The first law of friendship is sincerity.
Mutual attachment; intimacy. If not in friendship, live at least in peace.
Favor; personal kindness. His friendships, still a few confined, were always of the middling kind.
frieze: Properly, the nap on woolen cloth; hence, a kind of coarse woolen cloth or stuff, with a nap on one side.

In architecture, that part of the entablature of a column which is between the architrave and cornice. It is a flat member or face, usually enriched with figures of animals or other ornaments of sculpture, whence its name. Cornice or frieze with bossy sculptures graven.
friezed: Napped; shaggy with nap or frieze.
frigate: A ship of war, of a size larger than a sloop or brig, and less than a ship of the line; usually having two decks and carrying from thirty to forty four guns. But ships mounting a less number than thirty guns are sometimes called frigates; as are ships carrying a larger number.
Any small vessel on the water.
frigate-built: Having a quarter deck and forecastle raised above the main deck.
frigatoon: A Venetian vessel with a square stern, without a foremast, having only a mainmast and mizenmast.
fright: Sudden and violent fear; terror; a passion excited by the sudden appearance of danger. It expresses more than fear, and is distinguished from fear and dread, by its sudden invasion and temporary existence; fright being usually of short duration, whereas fear and dread may be long continued.
To terrify; to scare; to alarm suddenly with danger; to shock suddenly with the approach of evil; to daunt; to dismay. Nor exile or danger can fright a brave spirit.
frighten: To terrify; to scare; to alarm suddenly with danger; to shock suddenly with the approach of evil; to daunt; to dismay. Nor exile or danger can fright a brave spirit.
frightful: Terrible; dreadful; exciting alarm; impressing terror; as a frightful chasm or precipice; a frightful tempest.
frightfully: Terribly; dreadfully; in a manner to impress terror and alarm; horribly.
Very disagreeably; shockingly. She looks frightfully to day.
frigid: Cold; wanting heat or warmth; as the frigid zone.
Wanting warmth of affection; unfeeling; as a frigid temper or constitution.
Wanting natural heat or vigor sufficient to excite the generative power; impotent.
Dull; jejune; unanimated; wanting the fire of genius or fancy; as a frigid style; frigid rhymes.
Stiff; formal; forbidding; as a frigid look or manner.
frigidity: Coldness; want of warmth. But not applied to the air or weather.
Want of natural heat, life and vigor of body; impotency; imbecility; as the frigidity of old age.
Dullness; want of animation or intellectual fire; as the frigidity of sentiments or style.
frigidness: Coldness; dullness; want of heat or vigor; want of affection.
frigorific: Causing cold; producing or generating cold.
frill: An edging of fine linen on the bosom of a shirt or other similar thing; a ruffle.
To shake; to quake; to shiver as with cold; as, the hawk frills.
fringe: An ornamental appendage to the borders of garments or furniture, consisting of loose threads. The golden fringe ev’n set the ground on flame.
Something resembling fringe; an open broken border.
To adorn or border with fringe or a loose edging.
fripperer: One who deals in old cloths.
frippery: Old clothes; cast dresses; clothes thrown aside, after wearing. Hence, waste matter; useless things; trifles; as the frippery of wit.
The place where old clothes are sold.
The trade or traffic in old clothes.
frisk: To leap; to skip; to spring suddenly one way and the other. The fish fell a frisking in the net.
To dance, skip and gambol in frolic and gaiety. The frisking satyrs on the summits danced. In vain to frisk or climb he tries.
A frolic; a fit of wanton gaiety.
frisker: One who leaps or dances in gaiety; a wanton; an inconstant or unsettled person.
frisket: In printing, the light frame in which a sheet of paper is confined to be laid on the form for impression.
friskiness: Briskness and frequency of motion; gaiety; liveliness; a dancing or leaping in frolic.
frisking: Leaping; skipping; dancing about; moving with life and gaiety.
frit: In the manufacture of glass, the matter of which glass is made after it has been calcined or baked in a furnace. It is a composition of silex and fixed alkali, occasionally with other ingredients.
frith: Anarrow passage of the sea; a strait. It is used for the opening of a river into the sea; as the frith of Forth, or of Clyde.
A kind of wear for catching fish.
Asmall field taken out of a common.
fritillary: The crown imperial, a genus of plants, called in the Spanish dictionary checkered lily.
fritter: A small pancake; also, a small piece of meat fried.
A fragment; a shred; a small piece. And cut whole giants into fritters.
To cut meat into small pieces to be fried.
To break into small pieces or fragments. Break all their nerves, and fritter all their sense. To fritter away, is to diminish; to pare off; to reduce to nothing by taking away a little at a time.
frivolous: Slight; trifling; trivial; of little weight, worth or importance; not worth notice; as a frivolous argument; a frivolous objection or pretext.
frivolousness: The quality of being trifling or of very little worth or importance; want of consequence.
friz: To curl; to crisp to form into small curls with a crisping-pin.
To form the nap of cloth into little hard burs, prominences or knobs.
frized: Curled; formed into little burs on cloth.
frizing: Curling; forming little hard burs on cloth.
frizzle: To curl; to crisp; as hair.
fro: From; away; back or backward; as in the phrase, to and fro, that is, to and from, forward or toward and backward, hither and thither.
frock: An upper coat, or an outer garment. The word is now used for a loose garment or shirt worn by men over their other clothes, and for a kind of gown open behind, worn by females. The frock was formerly a garment worn by monks.
frog: An amphibious animal of the genus Rana, with four feet, a naked body, and without a tail. It is remarkable for swimming with rapidity, and for taking large leaps on land. Frogs lie torpid during winter.
frogfish: An animal of surinam, which is said to change from a fish to a frog and then to a fish again. It is cartilaginous, and exquisite food.
froise: A kind of food made by frying bacon inclosed in a pancake.
frolick: Gay; merry; full of levity; dancing, playing or frisking about; full of pranks. The frolick wind that breathes the spring. The gay, the frolick, and the loud.
A wild prank; a flight of levity, or gaiety and mirth. He would be at his frolick once again.
A scene of gaiety and mirth, as in dancing or play.
To play wild pranks; to play tricks of levity, mirth and gaiety. The buzzing insects frolick in the air.
frolicksome: Full of gaiety and mirth; given to pranks.
from: The sense of from may be expressed by the noun distance, or by the adjective distant, or by the participles, departing, removing to a distance. Thus it is one hundred miles from Boston to Hartford. He took his sword from his side. Light proceeds from the sun. Water issues from the earth in springs. Separate the coarse wool from the fine. Men have all sprung from Adam. Men often go from good to bad, and from bad to worse. The merit of an action depends on the principle from which it proceeds. Men judge of facts from personal knowledge, or from testimony. We should aim to judge from undeniable premises. The sense of from is literal or figurative, but it is uniformly the same. In certain phrases, generally or always elliptical, from is followed by certain adverbs, denoting place, region or position, indefinitely, no precise point being expressed; as, From above, from the upper regions. From
fromward: Away from; the contrary of toward.
frond: In botany, a term which Linne applies to the peculiar leafing of palms and ferns. He defines it, a kind of stem which has the branch united with the leaf and frequently with the fructification. The term seems to import the union of a leaf and a branch.
frondescence: In botany, the precise time of the year and month in which each species of plants unfolds its leaves.
frondous: A frondous flower is one which is leafy, one which produces branches charged with both leaves and flowers. Instances of this luxuriance sometimes occur in the rose and anemone.
front: Properly, the forehead, or part of the face above the eyes; hence, the whole face. His front yet threatens, and his frowns command.
The forehead or face, as expressive of the temper or disposition; as a fold front, equivalent to boldness or impudence. So a hardened front is shamelessness.
The forepart of any thing; as the front of a house, the principal face or side.
The forepart or van of an army or a body of troops.
The part or place before the face, or opposed to it, or to the forepart of a thing. He stood in front of his troops. The road passes in front of his house.
frontal: FRONTBOxThe box in a playhouse before the rest.
In medicine, a medicament or preparation to be applied to the forehead.
In architecture, a little pediment or frontpiece, over a small door or window.

In Jewish ceremonies, a frontlet or browband, consisting of four pieces of vellum, laid on leather, and tied round the forehead in the synagogue; each piece containing some text of scripture. FRONTBOXxXThe box in a playhouse before the rest.
frontier: The marches; the border, confine, or extreme part of a country, bordering on another country; that is, the part furthest advanced, or the part that fronts an enemy, or which an invading enemy meets in front, or which fronts another country.
Lying on the exterior part; bordering; conterminous; as a frontier town.
frontinac: A species of French wine, named from the place in Languedoc where it is produced.
frontiniac: A species of French wine, named from the place in Languedoc where it is produced.
frontispiece: In architecture, the principal face of a building; the face that directly presents itself to the eye.
An ornamental figure or engraving fronting the first page of a book, or at the beginning.
frontless: Wanting shame or modesty; not diffident; as frontless vice; frontless flattery.
frontlet: A frontal or browband; a fillet or band worn on the forehead. Deuteronomy 6:8.
frontroom: a room or apartment in the forepart of a house.
frory: Covered with a froth resembling hoarfrost.
frost: A fluid congealed by cold into ice or crystals; as hoar-frost, which is dew or vapor congealed. He scattereth the hoar-frost like ashes. Psalm 147:16.
The act of freezing; congelation of fluids. The third day comes a frost, a killing frost.
In physiology, that state or temperature of the air which occasions freezing or the coagelation of water.
The appearance of plants sparkling with icy crystals.
In cookery, to cover or sprinkle with a composition of sugar, resembling hoar-frost; as, to frost cake.
frostbitten: Nipped, withered or affected by frost.
frosted: Covered with a composition like white frost.
a. Having hair changed to a gray or white color, as if covered with hoar-frost; as a head frosted by age.
frostiness: The state or quality of being frosty; freezing cold.
frosting: the composition resembling hoar-frost, used to cover cake
frostless: Free from frost; as a frostless winter.
frostnail: a nail driven into a horse-shoe, to prevent the horse from slipping on ice. In some of the United States, the ends of the shoe are pointed for this purpose, and these points are called calks.
frosty: Producing frost; having power to congeal water; as a frosty night; frosty weather.
Containing frost; as, the grass is frosty.
Chill in affection; without warmth of affection or courage.
Resembling hoar-frost; white gray-haired; as a frosty head.
froth: Spume; foam; the bubbles causes in liquors by fermentation or agitation.
Any empty, senseless show of wit or eloquence.
To foam; to throw up spume; to throw out foam or bubbles. Beer froths in fermentation. The sea froths when violently agitated. A horse froths at the mouth when heated.
frothiness: The state of being frothy; emptiness; senseless matter.
frothy: Full of foam or froth, or consisting of froth or light bubbles.
Vain; light; empty; unsubstantial; as a vain frothy speaker; a frothy harangue.
frounce: A distemper of hawks, in which white spittle gathers about the bill.
To curl or frizzle the hair about the face. Not tricked and frounced as she was wont.
A wrinkle, plait or curl; an ornament of dress.
froward: Perverse, that is, turning from, with aversion or reluctance; not willing to yield or comply with what is required; unyielding; ungovernable; refractory; disobedient; peevish; as a froward child. They are a very froward generation, children in whom is no fair. Deuteronomy 32:20.
frowardness: Perverseness; reluctance to yield or comply; disobedience; peevishness.
frower: A sharp edged tool to cleave laths.
frown: To express displeasure by contracting the brow, and looking grim or surly; to look stern; followed by on or at; as, to frown on a profligate man, or to frown at his vices. Heroes in animated marble frown.
To manifest, displeasure in any manner. When providence frowns on our labors, let us be humble and submissive.
To repel by expressing displeasure; to rebuke. Frown the impudent fellow into silence.
A wrinkled look, particularly expressing dislike; a sour, severe or stern look, expressive of displeasure. His front yet threatens and his frowns command.
frowning: Knitting the brow in anger or displeasure; expressing displeasure by a surly, stern or angry look; lowering; threatening.
frowningly: Sternly; with a look of displeasure.
frowy: Musty; rancid; rank; as frowy butter.
frozen: of freeze. F.R.S. Fellow of the Royal Society.
Cold; frosty; chill; as the frozen climates of the north.
Void of natural heat or vigor. F.R.S. Fellow of the Royal Society.
fructescence: In botany, the precise time when the fruit of a plant arrives at maturity, and its seeds are dispersed; the fruiting season.
fructification: The act of fructifying, or rendering productive of fruit; fecundation.

In botany, the temporary part of a plant appropriated to generation, terminating the old vegetable and beginning the new. It consists of seven parts, the calyx, impalement or flower-cup, the corol or petals, the stamens, and the pistil, which belong to the flower, the pericarp and seed, which pertain to the fruit, and the receptacle or base, on which the other parts are seated. The receptacle belongs both to the flower and fruit.
fructify: To make fruitful; to render productive; to fertilize; as, to fructify the earth.
fructuous: Fruitful; fertile; also, impregnating with fertility.
frugal: Economical in the use or appropriation of money, goods or provisions of any kind; saving unnecessary expense, either of money or of any thing else which is to be used or consumed; sparing; not profuse, prodigal or lavish. We ought to be frugal not only in the expenditure of money and of goods, but in the employment of time. It is followed by of, before the thing saved; as frugal of time. It is not synonymous with parsimonious, nor with thrifty, as now used.
frugality: Prudent economy; good husbandry or housewifery; a sparing use or appropriation of money or commodities; a judicious use of any thing to be expended or employed; that careful management of money or goods which expends nothing unnecessarily, and applies what is used to a profitable purpose; that use in which nothing is wasted. It is not equivalent to parsimony, the latter being an excess of frugality, and a fault. Frugality is always a virtue. Nor is it synonymous with thrift, in its proper sense; for thrift is the effect of frugality: . Without frugality none can become rich, and with it few would be poor.
A prudent and sparing use or appropriation of any thing; as frugality of praise.
frugally: With economy; with good management; in a saving manner. He seldom lives frugally, that lives by chance.
frugivorous: Feeding on fruits, seeds or corn, as birds and other animals.
fruit: In a general sense, whatever the earth produces for the nourishment of animals, or for clothing or profit. Among the fruits of the earth are included not only corn of all kinds, but grass, cotton, flax, grapes and all cultivated plants. In this comprehensive sense, the word is generally used in the plural.

In a more limited sense, the produce of a tree or other plant; the last production for the propagation or multiplication of its kind; the seed of plants, or the part that contains the seeds; as wheat, rye, oats, apples, quinces, pears, cherries, acorns, melons.
In botany, the seed of a plant, or the seed with the pericarp.
Production; that which is produced. The fruit of the spirit is in all goodness, and righteousness, and truth. Ephesians 5:9.
The produce of animals; offspring; young; as the fruit of the womb, of the loins, of the body.
fruitbearing: Producing fruit; having the quality of bearing fruit.
fruiterer: One who deals in fruit; a seller of fruits.
fruitery: A fruitloft; a repository for fruit.
fruitful: Very productive; producing fruit in abundance; as fruitful soil; a fruitful tree; a fruitful season.
Prolific; bearing children; not barren. Be fruitful, and multiply - Genesis 1:22, 28.
Productive of any thing; fertile; as fruitful in expedients.
Producing in abundance; generating; as fruitful in crimes.
fruitfully: In such a manner as to be prolific.
fruitfulness: The quality of producing fruit in abundance; productiveness; fertility; as the fruitfulness of land.
Fecundity; the quality of being prolific, or producing many young; applied to animals.
Productiveness of the intellect; as the fruitfulness of the brain,
fruit-grove: A grove or close plantation of fruit trees.
fruition: Use accompanied with pleasure, corporeal or intellectual; enjoyment; the pleasure derived from use or possession. If the affliction is on his body, his appetites are weakened, and capacity of fruition destroyed.
fruitless: Not bearing fruit; barren; destitute of fruit; as a fruitless plant.
Productive of no advantage or good effect; vain; idle; useless; unprofitable; as a fruitless attempt; a fruitless controversy.
fruitlessly: Without any valuable effect; idly; vainly; unprofitably.
fruitlessness: The quality of being vain or unprofitable.
fruit-loft: A place for the preservation of fruit.
fruit-tree: A tree cultivated for its fruit, or a tree whose principal value consists in the fruit it produces, as the cherry tree, apple tree, pear tree. The oak and beech produce valuable fruit, but the fruit is not their principal value.
frumentaceous: Made of wheat, or like grain.
Resembling wheat, in respect to leaves, ears, fruit, and the like.
frumentation: Among the Romans, a largess of grain bestowed on the people to quiet them when uneasy or turbulent.
frumenty: Food made of wheat boiled in milk.
frush: In farriery, a sort of tender horn that grows in the middle of the sole of a horse, at some distance from the toe, dividing into two branches, and running toward the heel in the form of a fork.
frustrable: That may be frustrated or defeated.
frustrate: Literally, to break or interrupt; hence, to defeat; to disappoint; to balk; to bring to nothing; as, to frustrate a plan, design or attempt; to frustrate the will or purpose.
To make null; to nullify; to render of no effect; as, to frustrate a conveyance or deed.
Vain; ineffectual; useless; unprofitable; null; void; of no effect.
frustrated: Defeated; disappointed; rendered vain or null.
frustrating: Defeating; disappointing; making vain or of no effect.
frustration: The act of frustrating; disappointment; defeat; as the frustration of one’s attempt or design.
frustratory: That makes void; that vacates or renders null; as a frustatory appeal.
frustum: a piece or part of a solid body separated from the rest. The frustum of a cone, is the part that remains after the top is cut off by a plane parallel to the base; called otherwise a truncated cone.
frutescent: In botany, from herbaceous becoming shrubby; as a frutescent stem.
frutex: In botany, a shrub; a plant having a woody, durable stem, but less than a tree.
fry: To dress with fat by heating or roasting in a pan over a fire; to cook and prepare for eating in a fryingpan; as, to fry meat or vegetables.
To be heated and agitated; to suffer the action of fire or extreme heat.
To ferment, as in the stomach.
A swarm or crowd of little fish; so called from their crowding, tumbling and agitation.
frying: Dressing in a fryingpan; heating; agitating.
fryingpan: a pan with a long handle, used for frying meat and vegetables.
fub: to put off; to delay; to cheat.
fucate: Painted; disguised with paint; also, disguised with false show.
fucated: Painted; disguised with paint; also, disguised with false show.
fucus: A paint; a dye; also, false show.
plu. fucuses. In botany, a genus of Algae, or seaweed; the sea- wrack.
fuel: Any matter which serves as aliment to fire; that which feeds fire; combustible matter, as wood, coal, peat.
Any thing that serves to feed or increase flame, heat or excitement.
To feed with combustible matter. Never, alas! the dreadful name, that fuels the infernal flame.
To store with fuel or firing.
fueled: Fed with combustible matter; stored with firing.
fueler: He or that which supplies fuel.
fueling: Feeding with fuel; supplying with fuel.
fugaciousness: The quality of flying away; volatility.
fugacity: Volatility; the quality of flying away; as the fugacity of spires.
fugitive: Volatile; apt to flee away; readily wafted by the wind. The more tender and fugitive parts - .
Not tenable; not to be held or detained; readily escaping; as a fugitive idea.
Unstable; unsteady; fleeting; not fixed or durable.
Fleeing; running from danger or pursuit.
Fleeing from duty; eloping; escaping. Can a fugitive daughter enjoy herself, while her parents are in tears?
fugitiveness: Volatility; fugacity; an aptness to fly away.
fugue: In music, a chase or succession in the parts; that which expresses the capital thought or sentiment of the piece, in causing it to pass successively and alternately from one part to another.
fuguist: A musician who composes fugues, or performs them extemporaneously.
fulciment: A prop; a fulcrum; that on which a balance or lever rests.
fulcrate: In botany, a fulcrate stem is one whose branches descend to the earth, as in Ficus.
fulcrum: In mechanics, that by which a lever is sustained.
In botany, the part of a plant which serves to support or defend it, or to facilitate some necessary secretion, as a stipule, a bracte, a tendril, a gland.
fulcre: In mechanics, that by which a lever is sustained.
In botany, the part of a plant which serves to support or defend it, or to facilitate some necessary secretion, as a stipule, a bracte, a tendril, a gland.
fulfill: To accomplish; to perform; to complete; to answer in execution or event what has been foretold or promised; as, to fulfill a prophecy or prediction; to fulfill a promise.
To accomplish what was intended; to answer a design by execution. Here nature seems fulfilled in all her ends.
To accomplish or perform what was desired; to answer any desire by compliance or gratification. He will fulfill the desire of them that fear him. Psalm 145:19.
To perform what is required; to answer a law by obedience. If ye fulfill the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself, ye do well. James 2:8.
To complete in time. Fulfill her week. Genesis 29:27.
fulfilling: Accomplishment; completion; as the fulfillment of prophecy.
Execution; performance; as the fulfillment of a promise.
fulfillment: Accomplishment; completion; as the fulfillment of prophecy.
Execution; performance; as the fulfillment of a promise.
fuliginous: Pertaining to soot; sooty; dark; dusky.
Pertaining to smoke; resembling smoke; dusky.
full: Replete; having within its limits all that it can contain; as a vessel full of liquor.
Abounding with; having a large quantity or abundance; as a house full of furniture; life is full of cares and perplexities.
Supplied; not vacant. Had the throne been full, their meeting would not have been regular.
Plump; fat; as a full body.
Saturated; sated. I am full of the burnt offerings of rams. Isaiah 1:11.
full-acorned: Fed to the full with acorns.
full-bottom: A wig with a large bottom.
full-bottomed: Having a large bottom, as a wig.
full-eared: Having the ears or heads full of grain.
full-fed: Fed to fullness; plump with fat.
full-gorged: Over fed; a term of hawking.
full-hot: Quite as hot as it ought to be.
full-mouthed: Having a full or strong voice.
full-orbed: Having the orb complete or fully illuminated as the moon; like the full moon.
full-winged: Having complete wings or large strong wings.
fulled: Cleansed; thickened; made dense and firm in a mill.
fuller: One whose occupation is to full cloth.
fullery: The place or the works where the fulling of cloth is carried on.
fulling: Thickening cloth in a mill; making compact.
The art or practice of thickening cloth and making it compact and firm in a mill, at the same time the cloth is cleansed of oily matter.
fullingmill: A mill for fulling cloth by means of pestles or stampers, which beat and press it to a close or compact state and cleanse it.
fullness: The state of being filled, so as to leave no part vacant.
The state of abounding or being in great plenty; abundance.
Completeness; the state of a thing in which nothing is wanted; perfection. In thy presence is fullness of joy. Psalm 16:11.£
Repletion of vessels; as fullness of blood.
fullsome: Gross; disgusting by plainness, grossness or excess; as fullsome flattery or praise.
fullsomely: Grossly; with disgusting plainness or excess.
fully: Completely; entirely; without lack or defect; in a manner to give satisfaction; to the extent desired; as, to be fully persuaded of the truth of a proposition.
Completely; perfectly. Things partially known in this life will be hereafter fully disclosed.
fulmar: A fowl of the genus Procellaria, or petrel kind, larger than a gull, possessing the singular faculty of spouting from its bill a quantity of pure oil against its adversary. It is an inhabitant of the Hebrides; it feeds on the fat of whales, and when one of them is taken, will perch on it even when alive and pick out pieces of flesh.
fulminate: To make a loud sudden noise, or a sudden sharp crack; to detonate; as fulminating gold.
To hurl papal thunder; to issue forth ecclesiastical censures, as the pope.
To utter or send out, as a denunciation or censure; to send out, aS a menace or censure by ecclesiastical authority.
fluminating: Hurling papal denunciations, menaces or censures. Fulminating powder, a detonating compound of sulphur, carbonate of potash and niter.
fulmination: Denunciation of censure or threats, as by papal authority. The fulminations from the Vatican were turned into ridicule.
The explosion of certain chimical preparations; detonation.
fulminic: Fulminic acid, in chimistry, is a peculiar acid contained in fulminating silver.
fulsome: Nauseous; offensive. He that brings fulsome objects to my view, with nauseous images my fancy fills.
Rank; offensive to the smell; as a rank and fulsome smell.

Tending to obscenity; as a fulsome epigram. These are the English definitions of fulsome, but I have never witnessed such applications of the word in the United States. It seems then that full and foul are radically the same word, the primary sense of which is stuffed, crowded, from the sense of putting on or in. In the United States, the compound fullsome takes its signification from full, in the sense of cloying or satiating, and in England, fulsome takes its predominant sense from foulness.
fumatory: A plant or genus of plants, called Fumaria, of several species.
fumble: To feel or grope about; to attempt awkwardly.
To grope about in perplexity; to seek awkwardly; as, to fumble for an excuse.
To handle much; to play childishly; to turn over and over. I saw him fumble with the sheets, and play with flowers.
To manage awkwardly; to crowd or tumble together.
fumbler: One who gropes or manages awkwardly.
fume: Smoke; vapor from combustion, as from burning wood or tobacco.
Vapor; volatile matter ascending in a dense body.
Exhalation from the stomach; as the fumes of wine.
Rage; heat; as the fumes of passion.
fumigate: To apply smoke to; to expose to smoke; as in chimistry, or in medicine by inhaling it, or in cleansing infected apartments.
fumigation: The act of smoking or applying smoke, as in chimistry for softening a metal, or in the healing art by inhaling the smoke of certain substances. Expectoration is often assisted and sometimes ulcers of the lungs healed by fumigation. Fumigation is also used in cleansing infected rooms.
fumous: Producing fume; full of vapor. From dice and wine the youth retir’d to rest, and puffed the funny god from out his breast.
fumy: Producing fume; full of vapor. From dice and wine the youth retir’d to rest, and puffed the funny god from out his breast.
fun: Sport; vulgar merriment. A low word.
funambulatory: Performing like a rope dancer; narrow like the walk of a rope dancer.
function: In a general sense, the doing, executing or performing of any thing; discharge; performance; as the function of a calling or office. More generally,

Office or employment, or any duty or business belonging to a particular station or character, or required of a person in the station or character. Thus we speak of the functions of a chancellor, judge or bishop; the functions of a parent or guardian.

The office of any particular part of animal bodies; the peculiar or appropriate action of a member or part of the body, by which the animal economy is carried on. Thus we speak of the functions of the brain and nerves, of the heart, of the liver, of the muscles.
Power; faculty, animal or intellectual. As the mind opens, and its functions spread.
functionary: One who holds an office or trust; as a public functionary; secular functionaries.
fund: A stock or capital; a sum of money appropriated as the foundation of some commercial or other operation, undertaken with a view to profit, and by means of which expenses and credit are supported. Thus the capital stock of a banking institution is called its fund; the joint stock of a commercial or manufacturing house constitutes its fund or funds; and hence the word is applied to the money which an individual may possess, or the means he can employ for carrying on any enterprise or operation. No prudent man undertakes an expensive business without funds.

Money lent to government, constituting a national debt; or the stock of a national debt. Thus we say, a man is interested in the funds or public funds, when he owns the stock or the evidences of the public debt; and the funds are said to rise or fall, when a given amount of that debt sells for more or less in the market.
Money or income destined to the payment of the interest of a debt.
A sinking fund is a sum of money appropriated to the purchase of the public stocks or the payment of the public debt.
A stock or capital to afford supplies of any kind; as a fund of wisdom or good sense; a fund of wit. Hence.
fundament: The seat; the lower part of the body or of the intestinum rectum.
fundamental: Pertaining to the foundation or basis; serving for the foundation. Hence, essential; important; as a fundamental truth or principle; a fundamental law; a fundamental sound or chord in music.
A leading or primary principle, rule, law or article, which serves as the ground work of a system; essential part; as the fundamentals of the christian faith.
fundamentally: Primarily; originally; essentially; at the foundation. All power is fundamentally in the citizens of a state.
funded: Furnished with funds for regular payment of the interest of.
funding: Providing funds for the payment of the interest of.
funeral: Burial; the ceremony of buying a dead body; the solemnization of interment; obsequies.
The procession of persons attending the burial of the dead.
Pertaining to burial; used at the interment of the dead; as funeral rites, honors or ceremonies; a funeral torch; funeral feast or games; funeral oration.
funereal: Suiting a funeral; pertaining to burial.
fungate: A compound of fungic acid and a base.
fungic: Pertaining to or obtained from mushrooms; as fungic acid.
fungiform: In mineralogy, having a termination similar to the head of a fungus.
fun-gin: The fleshy part of mushrooms, now considered as a peculiar vegetable principle.
fungous: Like fungus or a mushroom; excrescent; spungy; soft.
Growing suddenly, but not substantial or durable.
fungus: A mushroom, vulgarly called a toadstool. The Fungi constitute an order of plants of a peculiar organization and manner of growth. The word is also applied to excrescences on plants.
A spungy excrescence in animal bodies as proud flesh formed in wounds. The term is particularly applied to any morbid excrescence, whether in wounds or arising spontaneously./ .
funicle: A small cord; a small ligature; a fiber.
funicular: Consisting of a small cord or fiber.
funnel: A passage or avenue for a fluid or flowing substance, particularly the shaft or hollow channel of a chimney through which smoke ascends.
A vessel for conveying fluids into close vessels; a kind of hollow cone with a pipe; a tunnel.
funnelform: Having the form of a funnel or inverted hollow cone.
funnelshaped: Having the form of a funnel or inverted hollow cone.
fur: The short, fine, soft hair of certain animals, growing thick on the skin, and distinguished from the hair, which is longer and coarser. Fur is one of the most perfect non-conductors of heat, and serves to keep animals warm in cold climates.
The skins of certain wild animals with the fur; peltry; as a cargo of furs.
Strips of skin with fur, used on garments for lining or for ornament. Garments are lined or faced with fur.
Hair in general; a loose application of the word.
A coat of morbid matter collected on the tongue in persons affected with fever.
furacious: Given to theft; inclined to steal; thievish.
furbelow: A piece of stuff plaited and puckered, on a gown or petticoat; a flounce; the plaited border of a petticoat or gown.
To put on a furbelow; to furnish with an ornamental appendage of dress.
furbish: To rub or scour to brightness; to polish; to burnish; as, to furbish a sword or spear; to furbish arms.
furbisher: One who polishes or makes bright by rubbing; one who cleans.
furcate: Forked; branching like the prongs of a fork.
furcation: A forking; a branching like the times of a fork.
furdle: To draw up into a bundle.
furious: Rushing with impetuosity; moving with violence; as a furious stream; a furious wind or storm.
Raging; violent; transported with passion; as a furious animal.
furiously: With impetuous motion or agitation; violently; vehemently; as, to run furiously; to attack one furiously.
furiousness: Impetuous motion or rushing; violent agitation.
furl: To draw up; to contract; to wrap or roll a sail close to the yard, stay or mast, and fasten it by a gasket or cord.
furled: Wrapped and fastened to a yard
furling: Wrapping or rolling and fastening to a yard
furlong: A measure of length; the eighth part of a mile; forty rods, poles or perches.
furlow: Leave of absence; a word used only in military affairs. Leave or license given by a commanding officer to an officer or soldier to be absent from service for a certain time.
To furnish with a furlow; to grant leave of absence to an officer or soldier.
furnace: A place where a vehement fire and heat may be made and maintained, for melting ores or metalsA furnace for casting cannon and other large operations is inclosed with walls through which a current of air is blown from a large bellows. In smaller operations a vessel is constructed with a chamber or cavity, with a door and a grate.
In scripture, a place of cruel bondage and affliction. Deuteronomy 4:20.
Grievous afflictions by which men are tried. Ezekiel 22:20-
A place of temporal torment. Danie! 3:6, 11, 15.
Hell; the place of endless torment. Matthew 13:42, 50.
furnish: To supply with any thing wanted or necessary; as, to furnish a family with provisions; to furnish arms for defense; to furnish a table; to furnish a library; to furnish one with money or implements.
To supply; to store; as, to furnish the mind with ideas; to furnish one with knowledge or principles.
To fit up; to supply with the proper goods, vessels or ornamental appendages; as, to furnish a house or a room.
To equip; to fit for an expedition; to supply.
furnisher: One who supplies or fits out.
furniture: Goods, vessels, utensils and other appendages necessary or convenient for housekeeping; whatever is added to the interior of a house or apartment, for use or convenience.
Appendages; that which is added for use or ornament; as the earth with all its furniture.
Equipage; ornaments; decorations; in a very general sense.
furred: Lined or ornamented with fur; thickened by the addition of a board.
furrier: A dealer in furs; one who makes or sells muffs, tippets
furring: Lining or ornamenting with fur; lining with a board.
furrow: A trench in the earth made by a plow.
A long narrow trench or channel in wood or metal; a groove.
A hollow made by wrinkles in the face.
To cut a furrow; to make furrows in; to plow.
To make long narrow channels or grooves in.
furrowfaced: Having a wrinkled or furrowed face.
furrowweed: A weed growing on plowed land.
furry: Covered with fur; dressed in fur.
Consisting of fur or skins; as furry spoils.
further: More or most distant; as the further end of the field.
Additional. We have a further reason for this opinion. We have nothing further to suggest. What further need have we of witnesses? Matthew 26:65.
To a greater distance. He went further.
To help forward; to promote; to advance onward; to forward; hence, to help or assist. This binds thee then to further my design.
furtherance: A helping forward; promotion; advancement. I know that I shall abide and continue with you all, for your furtherance and joy of faith. Philippians 1:25.
furtherer: One who helps to advance; a promoter.
furthermore: Moreover; besides; in addition to what has been said.
furthest: Most distant either in time or place.
furuncle: A small tumor or boil, with inflammation and pain, arising under the skin in the adipose membrane.
fury: A violent rushing; impetuous motion; as the fury of the winds.
Rage; a storm of anger; madness; turbulence. I do oppose my patience to his fury.
In mythology, a deity; a goddess of vengeance; hence, a stormy turbulent, violent woman.
furz: Gorse; whin; a thorny plant of the genus Ulex.
furzy: Overgrown with furz; full of gorse.
fuscite: A mineral of a grayish or greenish black color, found in Norway.
fuse: s as z. To melt; to liquefy by heat; to render fluid; to dissolve.
To be melted; to be reduced from a solid to a fluid state by heat.
fusee: s as z. The cone or conical part of a watch or clock, round which is wound the chain or cord.
A small neat musket or firelock. But we now use fusil.

Fusee or fuse of a bomb or granade, a small pipe filled with combustible matter by which fire is communicated to the powder in the bomb; but as the matter burns slowly, time is given before the charge takes fire, for the bomb to reach its destination.
fusibility: The quality of being fusible, or of being convertible from a solid to a fluid state by heat.
fusible: s as z. That may be melted or liquefied. The earths are found to be fusible.
fusil: Capable of being melted or rendered fluid by heat.
A bearing in heraldry of a rhomboidal figure, named from its shape, which resembles that of a spindle.
fusileer: Properly, a soldier armed with a fusil; but in modern times, a soldier armed like others of the infantry, and distinguished by wearing a cap like a grenadier, but somewhat shorter.
fusion: The act or operation of melting or rendering fluid by heat, without the aid of a solvent; as the fusion of ice or of metals.

The state of being melted or dissolved by heat; a state of fluidity or flowing in consequence of heat; as metals in fusion. Watery fusion, the melting of certain crystals by heat in their own water of crystallization.
fuss: A tumult; a bustle; but the word is vulgar.
fust: To become moldy; to smell ill.
fustet: The wood of the Rhus cotinus, which yields a fine orange color.
fustian: A kind of cotton stuff, or stuff of cotton and linen.

An inflated style of writing; a kind of writing in which high sounding words are used, above the dignity of the thoughts or subject; a swelling style; bombast. Fustian is thoughts and words ill sorted.
In style, swelling above the dignity of the thoughts or subject; too pompous; ridiculously tumid; bombastic.
fustic: The wood of the Morus tinctoria, a tree growing in the West Indies, imported and used in dyeing yellow.
fustigation: Among the Ancient Romans, a punishment by beating with a stick or club, inflicted on freemen.
fustiness: A fusty state or quality; an ill smell from moldiness, or moldiness itself.
fusty: Moldy; musty; ill smelling; rank; rancid. G, the seventh letter and the fifth articulation of the English Alphabet, is derived to us, through the Latin and Greek, from the Assyrian languages; it being found in the Chaldee, Syriac, Hebrew, Samaritan, Phenician, Ethiopic and Arabic. In the latter language, it is called giim or jim; ;but in the others, gimel, gomal or gamal, that is camel, from its shape. which resembles the neck of that animal, at least in the Chaldee and Hebrew. It is the third letter in the Chaldee, Syriac, Hebrew, Samaritan and Greek; the fifth in the Arabic, and the twentieth in the Ethiopic. The early Latins used C for the Greek gamma, and hence C came to hold the third place in the order of the Alphabet; the place which gimel holds in the oriental languages. fantonm: Something that appears to the imagination; also, a specter; a ghost; an apparition. It is generally written phantom, which see.
flaxcomb: An instrument with teeth through which flax is drawn for separating from it the tow or coarser part and the shives. In America, we call it a hatchel.
flees: To avoid; to keep at a distance from. FLEE. Flee fornication; flee from idolatry. 7 Corinthians 6:10.
fleshely: A fly that feeds on flesh, and deposits her eggs in it.
foredoonm: To doom beforehand; to predestinate. Thou art foredoomed to view the Stygian state
foreordann: To ordain or appoint before; to preordain; to predestinate; to predetermine.
futile: Trifling; of no weight or importance; answering no valuable purpose; worthless.
futility: Triflingness; unimportance; want of weight or effect; as, to expose the futility of arguments.
The quality of producing no valuable effect, or of coming to nothing; as the futility of measures or schemes.
futtock: In a ship, the futtocks are the middle timbers, between the floor and the upper timbers, or the timbers raised over the keel which form the breadth of the ship.
future: The future tense, in grammar, is the modification of a verb which expresses a future act or event.
That is to be or come hereafter; that will exist at any time after the presentThe next moment is future to the present.
Time to come; a time subsequent to the present; as, the future shall be as the present; in future; for the future. In such phrases, time or season is implied.
futurition: The state of being to come or exist hereafter.
futurity: Event to come. All futurities are naked before the all-seeing eye.
The state of being yet to come, or to come hereafter.
fuzz: To fly off in minute particles.
Fine, light particles; loose, volatile matter.
fuzzball: A kind of fungus or mushroom, which when pressed bursts and scatters a fine dust.
fy: A word which expresses blame, dislike, disapprobation, abhorrence or contempt. Fy, my lord, fy! a solider, and afraid! G

fins: To exhibit; to present or offer; as, to lay an indictment in a particular county. LAY.
To prostrate; to slay. LAY. The leaders first he laid along. LAY.
To depress and lose sight of, by sailing or departing from; as, to lay the land; a seaman’s phrase. LAY.
To station; to set; as, to lay an ambush. LAY.
To contrive; to scheme; to plan. LAY. To lay a cable, to twist or unite the strands. LAY. To lay apart, to put away; to reject. LAY. Lay apart all filthiness. James 1:21. LAY.
